topics.title: Densification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Densification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Densification** is a mechanical-physical process employed in waste and byproduct utilization engineering to convert loose, low-bulk-density, and high-porosity particulate materials (such as agricultural residues, sawdust, MSW fractions, forest biomass, and industrial byproducts) into compact, high-density, uniform-sized solid units known as **briquettes**, **pellets**, or **cubes**. The process applies axial or radial compressive force, often combined with controlled temperature and the addition of binders, to reduce inter-particle voids, plastically deform the lignocellulosic matrix, and form stable solid bonds (solid bridges, mechanical interlocking, and adhesive bonds) between adjacent particles.

The standard engineering metric of success is the **Compressive Strength** of the densified product, with industrial biomass briquettes typically rated between **$5 \text{ MPa}$** and **$12 \text{ MPa}$**, while premium pellets exceed **$15 \text{ MPa}$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (CO1, Module 1):**
> Densification is classified as a *tertiary conversion technology* under the broader umbrella of waste-to-energy processing. It directly addresses the operational challenges of storage, transport economics, and uniform combustion of dispersed biomass residues.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a shopping bag full of loose cotton. It is fluffy, takes up enormous volume, and would require a huge truck to transport even a small mass. Now imagine stuffing that same cotton into a small cardboard box and pressing it firmly under a hydraulic press. The volume collapses dramatically; the mass is now a single solid block that can be stacked, transported cheaply, and burned uniformly.

**That is densification in its purest form.**

In the context of waste utilization:
- The **loose cotton** = rice straw, sawdust, bagasse, groundnut shell, or shredded MSW with a bulk density of only **$60\text{–}150 \text{ kg/m}^3$**.
- The **pressed block** = a briquette with a density of **$1000\text{–}1300 \text{ kg/m}^3$**, ready for industrial combustion or gasification.

The engineering miracle here is that no chemical reaction is required — only mechanical compression. The natural binders within the biomass itself (lignin, hemicellulose, starches) become plastic when heated and act as the "glue" that holds the briquette together after cooling.

### 1.3 Why Densification Matters — The Three Engineering Drivers

1. **Logistical Economics (Transport Density):** The calorific value per unit volume of densified fuel is typically **3 to 7 times** higher than its loose form, drastically reducing hauling cost per GJ of energy delivered.
2. **Storage Geometry:** Briquettes can be stacked, reducing footprint by approximately **75%** compared to loose biomass.
3. **Process Uniformity:** Industrial boilers, gasifiers, and pyrolysers demand a consistent feed size and moisture content — only densification delivers both simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constant to Memorise:**
> The theoretical maximum densification ratio (compressed to original) achievable for most lignocellulosic biomass is approximately **$8 : 1$** to **$10 : 1$** under ideal conditions ($200^\circ C$, $100 \text{ MPa}$, optimal moisture).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The densification mechanism is governed by **five sequential phenomena** that occur during compression. Understanding these is essential for KTU examination answers worth 7–14 marks.

- **Step 1 — Particle Rearrangement (Low Pressure, 0–5 MPa):**
  Loose particles slide past one another, redistributing to fill inter-particle voids. Bulk density rises sharply with minimal applied force.

- **Step 2 — Elastic Deformation (5–25 MPa):**
  Particles deform elastically under load. Macropores collapse. The biomass behaves like a spring — releasing stored energy upon pressure removal (a key cause of briquette spring-back if not properly managed).

- **Step 3 — Plastic Deformation and Thermal Softening (25–100 MPa, 80–150°C):**
  Lignin, hemicellulose, and amorphous cellulose reach their glass transition temperature ($T_g$). They become viscoelastic and flow into remaining micropores, creating strong inter-particle solid bridges.

- **Step 4 — Solid Bridge Formation and Bond Activation (Cooling Stage):**
  As temperature drops below $T_g$, the softened lignin resolidifies, locking adjacent particles via:
  - Solid bridges (chemical bonds)
  - Mechanical interlocking (physical hooks)
  - Van der Waals attractions
  - Adhesive bonds (from any added binder)

- **Step 5 — Spring-Back / Relaxation (Post-Ejection):**
  Once the briquette is ejected, internal elastic stresses partially relax, causing a measurable dimensional expansion. High-quality densification minimizes this **spring-back ratio** to below **$2\%$**.

### 2.2 Classification of Densification Technologies

Densification equipment is broadly classified by the **direction of applied force** and the **operating temperature regime**.

- **Briquetting (Piston / Ram Press):** Reciprocating piston applies high axial pressure. Produces **log-shaped** briquettes of 50–90 mm diameter. Suitable for high-pressure work above **$50 \text{ MPa}$**.
- **Pelletizing (Roller Die / Ring Die Mill):** Material is forced through small cylindrical dies by rollers. Produces **uniform pellets** of 6–12 mm diameter.
- **Extrusion (Screw Extruder):** A rotating screw conveys and compresses material through a heated barrel. Operates in continuous mode. Common for **high-moisture biomass** and feedstocks with low lignin content.
- **Roll Press Briquetting (Compaction Grinder):** Two counter-rotating rolls press material into pillow-shaped briquettes. Best for medium-scale industrial production.
- **Cubing:** Compacts pre-conditioned biomass into small cubes. Often used for animal feed and densified fuel.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description / Engineering Utility |
|---|-----------|---------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | Densification Ratio (DR) | $DR = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{densified}}}{\rho_{\text{loose}}}$ | Dimensionless; target is typically $>6:1$ |
| 2 | Compressive Strength (CS) | $CS = \dfrac{F_{\max}}{A_{\text{cs}}}$ [MPa] | Load-bearing capacity of briquette cross-section |
| 3 | Durability Index (DI) | $DI = \dfrac{M_{\text{after}}}{M_{\text{before}}} \times 100$ [%] | Mass retained after tumbling/abrasion test (TUH standard) |
| 4 | Specific Energy Consumption | $E_s = \dfrac{W}{m}$ [kWh/tonne] | Energy required per tonne of feedstock |
| 5 | Moisture Reduction Ratio | $MRR = \dfrac{M_1 - M_2}{M_1}$ | Measures drying efficiency during pre-conditioning |
| 6 | Spring-Back Ratio | $SBR = \dfrac{L_{\text{after}} - L_{\text{before}}}{L_{\text{before}}} \times 100$ [%] | Expansion post-ejection; lower is better |
| 7 | Relaxed Density | $\rho_r = \dfrac{m}{V_{\text{after}}}$ | True operational density of finished product |
| 8 | Porosity | $\phi = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_s}$ | Fraction of void volume in densified product |
| 9 | Calorific Value per Unit Volume | $CV_v = CV_m \times \rho_b$ | Energy transport efficiency metric |
| 10 | Binder Dosage Ratio | $B\% = \dfrac{m_{\text{binder}}}{m_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Percentage of added binder by mass |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Warning:** In KTU answer sheets, always carry units through every calculation step. A $1\%$ slip on units is the most common reason for partial marking in 14-mark numerical problems.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Generation:** Co-firing densified biomass in coal-based thermal power plants to displace fossil fuel consumption.
- **Domestic Cooking:** Replacing firewood with briquettes reduces indoor air pollution by approximately **$60\%$**.
- **Industrial Boilers:** Uniform-sized briquettes enable automated feeding systems in textile, paper, and food processing industries.
- **Gasification & Pyrolysis Feedstock:** Controlled particle size and density improve reactor fluidization and conversion efficiency.
- **Carbonization Precursor:** Densified biomass is the preferred input for high-quality charcoal production via slow pyrolysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Compressive Strength from Compression Test Data

A standard KTU 14-mark problem requires deriving the **relaxed density** and **durability index** of a densified product. Below is the exhaustive derivation.

**Given:**
A cylindrical briquette of radius $r = 25 \text{ mm}$ and initial length $L_0 = 60 \text{ mm}$ is produced from biomass with a loose bulk density of $\rho_{\text{loose}} = 120 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The applied compressive load is $F = 12.36 \text{ kN}$. After release, the briquette relaxes to a length of $L_f = 32 \text{ mm}$. The mass of feedstock compressed is $m = 0.060 \text{ kg}$.

**Find:** Compressive Strength (CS), Densification Ratio (DR), and Spring-Back Ratio (SBR).

**Step 1 — Compute the cross-sectional area of the briquette**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{cs}} &= \pi r^2 \\
&= \pi \times (25 \times 10^{-3})^2 \\
&= \pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \\
&= 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert force to Newtons and compute Compressive Strength**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 12.36 \text{ kN} = 12360 \text{ N} \\
CS &= \dfrac{F}{A_{\text{cs}}} \\
&= \dfrac{12360}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= 6.295 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 6.30 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the volume of the relaxed briquette**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{relaxed}} &= A_{\text{cs}} \times L_f \\
&= 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 32 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 6.2832 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the relaxed density**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{relaxed}} &= \dfrac{m}{V_{\text{relaxed}}} \\
&= \dfrac{0.060}{6.2832 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= 954.93 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
&\approx 955 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Densification Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DR &= \dfrac{\rho_{\text{relaxed}}}{\rho_{\text{loose}}} \\
&= \dfrac{955}{120} \\
&= 7.96 \\
&\approx 8.0 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Spring-Back Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SBR &= \dfrac{L_0 - L_f}{L_0} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{60 - 32}{60} \times 100 \\
&= 46.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU Full-Mark Award:** A $SBR$ of $46.67\%$ indicates the compression is partially elastic — typical of cold-press briquetting. For industrial-grade durability, the process should be repeated or pre-heated to drop $SBR$ below $5\%$.

### 3.2 Derivation of Durability Index from Tumbling Test

**Given:** A sample of pellets is subjected to the TUH (Technische Universität Hamburg) tumbling test. Initial mass $= 500 \text{ g}$. After 500 revolutions at 50 rpm, retained mass $= 467 \text{ g}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DI &= \dfrac{M_{\text{after}}}{M_{\text{before}}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{467}{500} \times 100 \\
&= 93.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Acceptance Criterion:** Industrial-grade pellets require $DI \geq 95\%$. The above value indicates a borderline product — likely insufficient binder dosage.

### 3.3 Derivation of Specific Energy Consumption

**Given:** A piston briquetting press consumes $W = 18.75 \text{ kWh}$ per shift to produce $M = 2.5 \text{ tonnes}$ of briquettes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_s &= \dfrac{W}{M} \\
&= \dfrac{18.75}{2.5} \\
&= 7.5 \text{ kWh/tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Benchmark:** Commercial briquette plants typically operate between $7\text{–}12 \text{ kWh/tonne}$. Higher values suggest excessive friction losses in dies or sub-optimal feedstock moisture content.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram of Densification

Below is a Mermaid block-level functional architecture flow depicting the complete densification operation, from feedstock intake to finished product storage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Biomass Feedstock Intake] --> A2[Primary Size Reduction Shredder]
    A2 --> A3[Secondary Hammer Mill 5 mm Screen]
    A3 --> A4[Moisture Conditioning Chamber 8 to 12 percent]
    A4 --> A5[Optional Binder Addition Mixer]
    A5 --> A6[Pre-Heating Zone 80 to 150 degree C]
    A6 --> A7{Selection of Densification Technology}
    A7 --> B1[Piston Ram Briquetting]
    A7 --> B2[Roller Die Pellet Mill]
    A7 --> B3[Screw Extrusion]
    A7 --> B4[Roll Press Compaction]
    B1 --> C1[Cooling Conveyor]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Quality Testing Station Density and Durability]
    C2 --> C3[Storage Silo]
    C3 --> D1[Packaging and Dispatch]
    C2 --> D2[Reject Recycle Loop]
    D2 --> A3
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Influence of Process Variables

The diagram below illustrates the interrelationship between the four critical control parameters of densification and the resulting product quality.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pressure MPa] --> Q1[Product Density]
    P2[Temperature degree C] --> Q1
    P2 --> Q2[Durability Index]
    P3[Moisture Content percent] --> Q2
    P3 --> Q3[Specific Energy kWh per tonne]
    P4[Particle Size mm] --> Q1
    P4 --> Q3
    P5[Binding Agent Dosage] --> Q2
    P1 --> Q3
    Q1 --> R1[Final Briquette Quality]
    Q2 --> R1
    Q3 --> R1
```

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Cold Press vs Hot Press Densification

| Parameter | Cold Press Briquetting | Hot Press Briquetting |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Operating Temperature | $25\text{–}80^\circ C$ | $150\text{–}300^\circ C$ |
| Applied Pressure | $50\text{–}150 \text{ MPa}$ | $10\text{–}50 \text{ MPa}$ |
| Lignin Activation | Minimal — relies on binder | Full — natural lignin acts as binder |
| Binder Required | Yes (starch, molasses) | Optional |
| Energy Input | High mechanical | High thermal |
| Briquette Durability | Moderate | High |
| Typical Application | Charcoal production | Industrial fuel pellets |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Define densification. List any four types of densification processes used in waste-to-energy conversion.**

**Model Answer:**
Densification is the mechanical process of compacting loose, low-density particulate waste or biomass into high-density, uniform-sized solid fuel units by applying pressure, with or without heat, to form strong inter-particle bonds.

Four types of densification processes:
1. Piston / ram briquetting
2. Roller die pelletizing
3. Screw extrusion
4. Roll press compaction

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**Explain the role of lignin in biomass densification.**

**Model Answer:**
Lignin is a natural thermoplastic polymer present in lignocellulosic biomass. When heated above its glass transition temperature ($T_g \approx 120\text{–}150^\circ C$), it softens, flows into micropores between particles, and acts as a natural binder. Upon cooling, lignin resolidifies to form strong solid bridges that lock the particles together. The lignin content of a feedstock is therefore directly proportional to the durability of the densified product.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (KTU ESE Pattern):** Answer **any one** full question from each module. Each question carries **14 marks**, divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat flow diagram, explain the steps involved in the densification of agricultural residues into briquettes. Discuss the role of moisture content, particle size, and pressure on briquette quality.

**Model Answer:**

The densification of agricultural residues (such as rice straw, bagasse, groundnut shell, and cotton stalk) into briquettes follows a systematic sequence of operations:

**Step 1 — Collection and Sorting:**
Agricultural residues are gathered from farms, agro-industries, and food processing units. Foreign matter (stones, metal, plastic) is removed manually or via magnetic separators.

**Step 2 — Size Reduction:**
The residue is first passed through a shredder (course cutting to 10–30 mm) and then through a hammer mill with a 5–8 mm screen to obtain fine, uniform particles. Smaller particles increase inter-particle contact area, improving briquette strength.

**Step 3 — Moisture Conditioning:**
Moisture content is the single most influential parameter. The optimal range for most biomass is **$8\%$ to $12\%$** (wet basis). Too dry (below $6\%$) — particles do not deform and bond; too wet (above $15\%$) — steam explosions eject the briquette from the die and energy is wasted in evaporating water.

**Step 4 — Pre-heating:**
The conditioned biomass is heated to $80\text{–}150^\circ C$ to activate lignin and reduce spring-back.

**Step 5 — Compression:**
The material is fed into a briquetting press where pressures of $50\text{–}150 \text{ MPa}$ are applied. Piston presses deliver axial pressure; roller die mills force material radially through cylindrical dies.

**Step 6 — Cooling and Curing:**
Hot briquettes are cooled on a conveyor for $5\text{–}15 \text{ minutes}$ to allow lignin resolidification and to gain final mechanical strength.

**Step 7 — Quality Testing and Storage:**
Samples are tested for density, durability, moisture, and calorific value before dispatch.

**Influence of Key Parameters:**

| Parameter | Effect on Briquette Quality |
|-----------|------------------------------|
| **Moisture Content** | Optimal at $8$–$12\%$; produces maximum density and durability |
| **Particle Size** | Finer particles ($<5 \text{ mm}$) give higher density due to better packing |
| **Pressure** | Higher pressure (up to $\sim 120 \text{ MPa}$) increases density but increases specific energy consumption |
| **Temperature** | Pre-heating to $>120^\circ C$ activates lignin, reducing binder requirement |

**(b) [7 Marks]** A piston briquetting press operates at $100 \text{ MPa}$ pressure on biomass with a loose bulk density of $130 \text{ kg/m}^3$. If the resulting briquette has a relaxed density of $1100 \text{ kg/m}^3$, determine (i) the Densification Ratio, and (ii) the volume reduction achieved per kg of feedstock. Comment on whether this densification is acceptable for industrial fuel applications.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Densification Ratio (DR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DR &= \dfrac{\rho_{\text{relaxed}}}{\rho_{\text{loose}}} \\
&= \dfrac{1100}{130} \\
&= 8.46 \\
&\approx 8.5 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Volume Reduction per kg of Feedstock:**

Loose volume per kg:
$$
V_{\text{loose}} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_{\text{loose}}} = \dfrac{1}{130} = 7.692 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

Densified volume per kg:
$$
V_{\text{densified}} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_{\text{relaxed}}} = \dfrac{1}{1100} = 9.091 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

Volume reduction per kg:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= V_{\text{loose}} - V_{\text{densified}} \\
&= 7.692 \times 10^{-3} - 9.091 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 6.783 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Percentage volume reduction:
$$
\%VR = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_{\text{loose}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{6.783 \times 10^{-3}}{7.692 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100 = 88.18\%
$$

**Engineering Comment:**
The achieved $DR$ of $8.5 : 1$ and a volume reduction of $88.2\%$ are within the **industrial benchmark range** (acceptable $DR > 6$, volume reduction $>85\%$). This densification is **acceptable for industrial fuel applications** such as co-firing in coal-based thermal plants or industrial boilers.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare briquetting, pelletizing, and extrusion as densification technologies. Discuss their suitability for different categories of biomass feedstock.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Briquetting | Pelletizing | Extrusion |
|---------|-------------|-------------|-----------|
| **Operating Pressure** | $50\text{–}150 \text{ MPa}$ | $15\text{–}40 \text{ MPa}$ | $5\text{–}30 \text{ MPa}$ |
| **Operating Temperature** | Ambient to $300^\circ C$ | $80\text{–}120^\circ C$ | $150\text{–}350^\circ C$ |
| **Product Shape** | Log / pillow | Uniform cylinder 6–12 mm | Continuous rod / sausage |
| **Product Size** | $50\text{–}90 \text{ mm}$ diameter | $6\text{–}12 \text{ mm}$ diameter | $15\text{–}75 \text{ mm}$ diameter |
| **Production Rate** | Low to medium | High | Medium to high |
| **Feedstock Moisture Tolerance** | Low (must be $<12\%$) | Low (must be $<12\%$) | **High (up to $35\%$)** |
| **Capital Cost** | High | Medium | Medium |
| **Energy Consumption** | High | Medium | Medium |

**Suitability by Feedstock:**

- **Briquetting** is best suited for **hard, woody biomass** such as sawdust, bark, and coconut shell, where high pressure and large product format are acceptable.
- **Pelletizing** is ideal for **uniform, fine, dry biomass** like sawdust, grass, and straw. It produces the highest quality fuel pellets used in domestic and small commercial boilers.
- **Extrusion** is uniquely capable of handling **high-moisture feedstock** such as wet MSW, sludge, and manure, because the heated barrel drives off water during processing.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A biomass pellet mill has the following specifications: Die diameter $= 8 \text{ mm}$, pellet length $= 25 \text{ mm}$, mass per pellet $= 1.2 \text{ g}$. Calculate (i) the volume and relaxed density of a single pellet, and (ii) the number of pellets that can be produced from one tonne of feedstock if the yield is $92\%$. Comment on the density value.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Volume and Relaxed Density of a Single Pellet:**

The pellet is treated as a cylinder of diameter $d = 8 \text{ mm}$ and length $L = 25 \text{ mm}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{pellet}} &= \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \times L \\
&= \dfrac{\pi (8 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} \times (25 \times 10^{-3}) \\
&= \dfrac{\pi \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5}}{4} \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 5.0265 \times 10^{-5} \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Relaxed density:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{pellet}} &= \dfrac{m}{V_{\text{pellet}}} \\
&= \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 954.93 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
&\approx 955 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Number of Pellets from 1 Tonne (with $92\%$ yield):**

Effective feedstock mass available for pelletization:
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{effective}} &= 1000 \text{ kg} \times 0.92 = 920 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Number of pellets:
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{m_{\text{effective}}}{m_{\text{pellet}}} \\
&= \dfrac{920}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= 766{,}667 \text{ pellets}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Comment on Density:**
A pellet density of **$955 \text{ kg/m}^3$** falls slightly below the **ENplus-A1 premium grade standard** ($>1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$). This suggests the die compression ratio is suboptimal or feedstock moisture content is slightly above the ideal $10\%$. Adjustment of die length-to-diameter ratio ($L/D > 4$ is recommended) and tighter drying control would elevate the product to premium grade.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Forgetting to specify "wet basis" or "dry basis"** when quoting moisture content — instant deduction of 0.5 marks.
> - **Mixing units** (e.g., using cm³ instead of m³ in density calculations) — common error, especially in 7-mark sub-parts.
> - **Omitting the role of lignin activation** in describing hot-press briquetting — losing the "process understanding" mark.
> - **Not stating the engineering benchmark** when commenting on derived results — examiners explicitly reward students who contextualize numerical answers against industrial standards.
> - **In diagrams, omitting arrows or flow direction** in the process flow — KTU explicitly awards 1 mark for a "properly labeled flow diagram" in 7-mark sub-parts.

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ Densification is a **physical-mechanical** process — not a chemical one.
- ✅ Three primary drivers: **transport economics, storage geometry, combustion uniformity.**
- ✅ Four major equipment families: **briquetting, pelletizing, extrusion, roll press.**
- ✅ Five-step mechanism: **rearrangement → elastic deformation → plastic deformation → bond formation → spring-back.**
- ✅ **Lignin activation at $120\text{–}150^\circ C$** is the natural binding mechanism in hot-press processes.
- ✅ Optimal moisture content for most biomass is **$8\%$ to $12\%$ wet basis.**
- ✅ Particle size target: **$<5 \text{ mm}$** for high-density pellets.
- ✅ Densification Ratio target: **$>6:1$**; Industrial benchmark: **$8:1$.**
- ✅ Durability Index: **$>95\%$** for premium grade (ENplus-A1).
- ✅ Compressive Strength: **$5\text{–}15 \text{ MPa}$** for industrial briquettes.
- ✅ Specific Energy Consumption: **$7\text{–}12 \text{ kWh/tonne}$** for commercial briquetting plants.
- ✅ Spring-Back Ratio should be **$<5\%$** for stable, durable briquettes.
- ✅ **Always carry units** through every calculation; **always state engineering benchmarks** for context.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip (CO1, Module 1):** When a 14-mark question asks about densification, structure your answer in three parts — **(1) Definition + Principle, (2) Process Flow + Parameters, (3) Numerical / Comparative Analysis**. This mapping directly satisfies the KTU 2024 outcome-based rubric and ensures every CO attainment indicator is met.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combustion and gasification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Combustion and gasification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Combustion?

**Combustion** is an exothermic, high-temperature, oxidation-reduction chemical reaction between a fuel (in our case, **municipal solid waste, agricultural residue, or industrial byproduct**) and an oxidizer (typically atmospheric air or pure oxygen), releasing heat, light, and **flue gas products** such as CO₂, H₂O, and SO₂.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Combustion is the rapid, high-temperature chemical combination of combustible elements (C, H, S) in waste with oxygen, releasing thermal energy that can be recovered for steam generation, district heating, or electricity production.*

The general simplified reaction for hydrocarbon waste can be written as:

$$\text{Fuel (C, H, S, O, N)} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{SO}_2 + \text{N}_2 + \text{Heat}$$

### Conceptual Analogy — The Campfire Mental Model

Imagine lighting a **campfire** with wood. The wood (fuel) needs three things to burn brightly:
- **Temperature** (initial spark to ignite)
- **Time** (residence of fuel in the hot zone)
- **Turbulence** (mixing of air with fuel vapors)

These are famously called the **"3Ts" of Combustion** in waste incineration engineering. If any one of the 3Ts is poor, you get **smoke, unburnt carbon (char), and pollution** — just like a smoky, smoldering campfire that wastes wood and pollutes the air. A well-designed **mass-burn incinerator** simply scales this campfire up to 850–1100 °C with engineered air injection.

## 1.2 What is Gasification?

**Gasification** is a thermochemical conversion process in which a carbonaceous feedstock (biomass, MSW, refuse-derived fuel) is partially oxidized at **sub-stoichiometric oxygen levels** (typically **20–40 %** of the theoretical oxygen required for complete combustion) to produce a **synthesis gas (syngas)** — a combustible mixture primarily composed of **CO, H₂, CH₄, CO₂, N₂**, and trace hydrocarbons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The defining mathematical parameter is the **Equivalence Ratio (ER)**:
> $$\text{ER} = \frac{\text{Actual Air/Fuel ratio}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air/Fuel ratio}}$$
> - **Combustion:** ER ≥ 1.0 (excess air)
> - **Gasification:** 0.20 ≤ ER ≤ 0.40 (oxygen-starved)
> - **Pyrolysis:** ER = 0 (no oxidant)

### Conceptual Analogy — The Charcoal Pit Mental Model

Picture a traditional village **charcoal pit** covered with soil and grass. The wood inside does NOT burst into flames (no excess oxygen), but slowly smolders in oxygen-starved conditions, releasing combustible gases that villagers use for cooking. This is **gasification in its oldest form** — converting solid fuel into a gaseous fuel. Modern gasifiers simply mechanize, control, and optimize this age-old process to produce clean **syngas** for engines, turbines, or chemical synthesis (Fischer-Tropsch diesel, methanol).

## 1.3 Why These Processes Matter for KTU

- Kerala generates over **5,000 tonnes/day of MSW**, of which only a fraction is scientifically treated.
- The Indian **Swachh Bharat Mission** and **Solid Waste Management Rules 2016** mandate thermal processing for non-recyclable, non-compostable waste.
- Combustion produces **0.6–1.0 MWh/tonne** of energy; gasification produces syngas suitable for reciprocating engines at **25–40 % electrical efficiency**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key distinguishing feature:** Combustion's *goal* is **heat**; Gasification's *goal* is **a gaseous fuel** that can be cleaned, stored, and flexibly used.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Combustion Process — Step-by-Step Logic

When a solid waste particle enters a furnace, it undergoes a **sequence of physico-chemical stages** that must be understood before any design calculation is attempted:

1. **Drying Stage (100–200 °C):** Free and bound moisture is vaporized. Latent heat required: ~2,260 kJ/kg of water evaporated. **Why:** Wet waste = efficiency loss. Keralite MSW with **30–50 % moisture** drastically reduces flame stability.
2. **Pyrolysis/Devolatilization (200–600 °C):** Volatiles (light hydrocarbons, tars, light oils) are released as gases; char (fixed carbon + ash) is left behind. **Why:** This stage determines the *ignition* behaviour of the waste.
3. **Combustion of Volatiles (600–900 °C):** Volatile gases mix with O₂ and burn in a diffusion flame. This is the dominant energy-release stage.
4. **Combustion of Fixed Carbon / Char (700–1100 °C):** Heterogeneous surface reaction:  
   $$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad (\Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol})$$
5. **Gas-Phase Reduction Zone (above char bed, in gasifiers only):**  
   $$\text{CO}_2 + \text{C} \rightarrow 2\text{CO} \quad (\text{Boudouard reaction})$$
   $$\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{C} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad (\text{Water-gas reaction})$$

## 2.2 The Gasification Process — Step-by-Step Logic

A gasifier contains **four reaction zones** in series (or co-existing in fluidized beds):

1. **Drying Zone** — moisture removal.
2. **Pyrolysis Zone** — volatile release, char formation.
3. **Oxidation (Combustion) Zone** — partial combustion of char with limited O₂, generating heat.
4. **Reduction Zone** — endothermic reactions between hot char and gasification products (CO₂, H₂O), converting them into **CO and H₂**.

> [!NOTE]
> The heat generated in the oxidation zone drives the endothermic reduction zone. This **internal heat balance** is the key to gasifier design stability.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units / Typical Value | KTU Relevance |
|---|---------------------|----------|------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Stoichiometric O₂ for carbon** | $\text{O}_2 = \frac{32}{12} \times \%\text{C} = 2.667\,C$ | kg O₂ / kg fuel | 2-mark derivations |
| 2 | **Stoichiometric O₂ for hydrogen** | $\text{O}_2 = \frac{32}{4} \times \%\text{H} = 8.0\,H$ | kg O₂ / kg fuel | 2-mark derivations |
| 3 | **Stoichiometric O₂ for sulphur** | $\text{O}_2 = \frac{32}{32} \times \%\text{S} = 1.0\,S$ | kg O₂ / kg fuel | 2-mark derivations |
| 4 | **Net O₂ required (subtracting fuel oxygen)** | $\text{O}_2 = 2.667\,C + 8.0\,H + 1.0\,S - 1.0\,O$ | kg O₂ / kg fuel | **Most asked** |
| 5 | **Theoretical (stoichiometric) air** | $A_{\text{th}} = \frac{\text{O}_2}{0.232}$ | kg air / kg fuel | 0.232 = mass fraction of O₂ in air |
| 6 | **Actual air supplied** | $A_{\text{act}} = A_{\text{th}} \times (1 + EA)$ | kg air / kg fuel | EA = excess air fraction |
| 7 | **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** | $\text{AFR} = \frac{A_{\text{act}}}{\text{Fuel mass}}$ | dimensionless | 5–10 typical for MSW |
| 8 | **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** | $\text{ER} = A_{\text{act}} / A_{\text{th}}$ | dimensionless | ER < 1 → fuel-rich |
| 9 | **Dulong's formula (HHV)** | $\text{HHV} = 0.338\,C + 1.428\,H + 0.095\,S - 0.108\,O$ | MJ / kg dry fuel | **Universally asked** |
| 10 | **Lower Heating Value** | $\text{LHV} = \text{HHV} - 2.442 \times (9H)$ | MJ / kg | 2.442 MJ/kg = latent heat of water |
| 11 | **Combustion Efficiency** | $\eta_c = \frac{\text{Heat released}}{\text{HHV} \times m_f}$ | % (typically 85–95 %) | KTU numerical problems |
| 12 | **Gasification Efficiency (Cold-Gas)** | $\eta_{cg} = \frac{\dot{m}_g \cdot \text{LHV}_g}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{HHV}_f}$ | % (typically 60–80 %) | Direct-asks in exam |
| 13 | **Carbon Conversion Efficiency** | $X_C = \frac{\text{C in gas}}{\text{C in fuel}}$ | % | 2-mark theory |
| 14 | **Boudouard equilibrium constant** | $K_p = \frac{P_{\text{CO}}^2}{P_{\text{CO}_2}}$ | bar | Reactor design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU numerical problems, **C, H, S, O** in formulas denote the **mass fractions** of carbon, hydrogen, sulphur, and oxygen in the *dry* waste. Moisture content is handled separately in the LHV step.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Combustion** is used in **mass-burn incinerators with energy recovery (MBT plants)** such as the **Kerala Enviro Infrastructure Ltd. (KEIL) facility at Brahmapuram, Kochi** — although it currently operates sub-optimally, KTU expects students to design for **>850 °C / >2 s residence time** to destroy dioxins.
- **Gasification** powers **diesel-substitute syngas engines** in rice-husk-based industries in Kuttanad and Kole lands, and in **tyre-pyrolysis plants** that recover carbon black from scrap tyres.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Oxygen Requirement for a Generic Waste

### Problem Statement (KTU Typical 7-Mark)

> A solid waste sample has the following ultimate analysis (by mass, dry basis):  
> C = 42 %, H = 6 %, S = 1 %, O = 28 %, N = 3 %, Ash = 20 %.  
> Moisture (as received) = 35 %.  
> Calculate (a) the stoichiometric oxygen required, (b) the theoretical air required, and (c) the actual air supplied at **40 % excess air**. Comment on the suitability of the AFR for MSW combustion.

### Step 1 — Write the ultimate analysis on a per-kg dry basis

We work on **1 kg of dry waste**:

$$
C = 0.42\ \text{kg}, \quad H = 0.06\ \text{kg}, \quad S = 0.01\ \text{kg}, \quad O = 0.28\ \text{kg}
$$

### Step 2 — Write the four combustion reactions

$$
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1\ \text{kg C needs } \frac{32}{12}\ \text{kg O}_2 = 2.667\ \text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1\ \text{kg H needs } \frac{32}{4}\ \text{kg O}_2 = 8.000\ \text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{SO}_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1\ \text{kg S needs } \frac{32}{32}\ \text{kg O}_2 = 1.000\ \text{kg}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute gross O₂ demand

$$
O_{2,\text{gross}} = (2.667 \times 0.42) + (8.000 \times 0.06) + (1.000 \times 0.01)
$$

$$
O_{2,\text{gross}} = 1.120 + 0.480 + 0.010 = 1.610\ \text{kg O}_2\ /\ \text{kg dry fuel}
$$

**Textual Logic:** We multiplied each element mass by the *kg of O₂ required to fully combust 1 kg of that element*. The sum gives the gross oxygen demand assuming no internal oxygen is present.

### Step 4 — Subtract the fuel-bound oxygen

The fuel's own oxygen (28 % of 1 kg = 0.28 kg) is *already* combined with hydrogen (as –OH groups, in cellulose, etc.) and is therefore not available for O₂. We subtract the *oxygen-equivalent already bound inside the fuel*:

$$
O_{2,\text{net}} = 1.610 - 0.280 = 1.330\ \text{kg O}_2\ /\ \text{kg dry fuel}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to theoretical air

Air contains 23.2 % O₂ by mass (the rest is N₂ ≈ 76.8 % + Ar). Therefore:

$$
A_{\text{th}} = \frac{O_{2,\text{net}}}{0.232} = \frac{1.330}{0.232} = 5.733\ \text{kg air / kg dry fuel}
$$

### Step 6 — Add excess air (40 %)

Excess air **EA = 0.40**, so:

$$
A_{\text{act}} = A_{\text{th}} \times (1 + 0.40) = 5.733 \times 1.40 = 8.026\ \text{kg air / kg dry fuel}
$$

### Step 7 — Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)

$$
\text{AFR} = \frac{A_{\text{act}}}{1\ \text{kg dry fuel}} = 8.026
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For typical high-moisture MSW, AFR lies in the range **5–10**, and this result lies in that range — confirming the calculated values are physically sensible. *(Low-rank coals have AFR 5–6; dry wood ~6; high-moisture MSW ~7–10.)*

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Dulong's Formula and the Higher Heating Value

### Step 1 — Calorific contribution of each element

Each combustible element (C, H, S) releases a fixed amount of heat per kg when burned completely, but the heat released per kg of *oxygen* consumed in the reaction is approximately the same (~24 MJ/kg O₂). Dulong observed that the HHV can therefore be written as a sum of the contributions from each element, **minus** the energy that did NOT go into combustion because of bound oxygen.

### Step 2 — Empirical constants (derived from standard heats of combustion)

Using the standard enthalpies of combustion of carbon, hydrogen, and sulphur and dividing by their atomic masses, we get the empirical constants used in KTU formulae:

$$
\text{For Carbon:} \quad \frac{393.5\ \text{kJ/mol}}{12\ \text{kg/kmol}} = 32.79\ \text{MJ/kg C} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{32.79}{97} \approx 0.338
$$

$$
\text{For Hydrogen:} \quad \frac{285.8\ \text{kJ/mol}}{2\ \text{kg/kmol}} = 142.9\ \text{MJ/kg H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{142.9}{100} \approx 1.428
$$

$$
\text{For Sulphur:} \quad \frac{296.8\ \text{kJ/mol}}{32\ \text{kg/kmol}} = 9.28\ \text{MJ/kg S} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{9.28}{97.5} \approx 0.095
$$

$$
\text{Correction for O:} \quad \text{−0.108 (empirical)}
$$

### Step 3 — Dulong's empirical formula

$$
\text{HHV} = 0.338\,C + 1.428\,H + 0.095\,S - 0.108\,O \quad \text{[MJ / kg dry fuel]}
$$

For our waste sample:

$$
\text{HHV} = (0.338 \times 42) + (1.428 \times 6) + (0.095 \times 1) - (0.108 \times 28)
$$

$$
\text{HHV} = 14.196 + 8.568 + 0.095 - 3.024 = 19.835\ \text{MJ/kg dry basis}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to "as received" (wet) basis

As-received HHV = HHV (dry) × (1 − moisture fraction)

$$
\text{HHV}_{\text{ar}} = 19.835 \times (1 - 0.35) = 19.835 \times 0.65 = 12.893\ \text{MJ/kg}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lower Heating Value (LHV) and Why It Matters in Gasifiers

The HHV assumes that the water produced by H-combustion condenses to liquid, releasing its latent heat of vaporization (2,442 kJ/kg water at 25 °C). In **incinerator flue gas** and **syngas streams**, the water leaves as **vapour** at high temperature. The LHV removes this latent heat.

### Step 1 — Water produced per kg of fuel

Each kg of H produces $\frac{18}{2} = 9$ kg of H₂O on combustion.

### Step 2 — Subtract the latent heat

$$
\text{LHV} = \text{HHV} - 9\,H \times 2.442
$$

For our sample (using dry H = 0.06 kg):

$$
\text{LHV}_{\text{dry}} = 19.835 - (9 \times 0.06 \times 2.442) = 19.835 - 1.319 = 18.516\ \text{MJ/kg dry}
$$

$$
\text{LHV}_{\text{ar}} = 18.516 \times 0.65 = 12.035\ \text{MJ/kg}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In India, **boiler efficiency is conventionally reported on an LHV basis**. In EU and US, it is often **HHV basis**. KTU expects LHV unless the question explicitly states HHV.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Combustion vs Gasification Parameter Comparison

| Parameter | Combustion | Gasification |
|---|---|---|
| Equivalence Ratio (ER) | 1.1 – 2.0 | 0.20 – 0.40 |
| Operating Temperature | 850 – 1200 °C | 600 – 900 °C |
| Main Product | Heat + Flue gas (CO₂, H₂O) | **Syngas** (CO, H₂, CH₄) |
| Secondary Product | Bottom ash, fly ash | Char, tars, ash |
| Air/Fuel Ratio (typical MSW) | 5 – 8 | 1.0 – 2.0 |
| Energy Conversion Efficiency | 25 – 35 % (electric) | 30 – 45 % (electric via engine) |
| NOx Emissions | High (thermal + fuel) | Lower (reducing atmosphere) |
| Capital Cost | Moderate | Higher (gas cleanup) |
| Air Pollution Control | Full flue-gas train | Tar reformer + gas scrubber |
| Best Use | Large-scale MSW, heat | Distributed power, chemicals |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU insight:** When asked *"Which process is preferred for high-moisture Kerala MSW?"* the correct engineering answer is **gasification followed by drying**, because combustion of 50 %-moisture waste has a poor energy balance — the latent-heat loss in the flue gas dominates.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Combustion Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Waste Feed Hopper] --> B[Feed Chute and Ram Feeder]
    B --> C[Primary Combustion Chamber]
    C --> D[Secondary Combustion Chamber at 850 to 1100 C]
    D --> E[Heat Recovery Boiler and Steam Drum]
    E --> F[Steam Turbine and Generator]
    F --> G[Electrostatic Precipitator]
    G --> H[Bag Filter for Particulates]
    H --> I[Wet Scrubber for Acid Gases SO2 HCl]
    I --> J[Selective Catalytic Reduction for NOx]
    J --> K[ID Fan and Stack]
    E --> L[Ash Extraction System]
    L --> M[Bottom Ash and Fly Ash Silos]
    M --> N[Ash Disposal or Brick Making]
    E --> O[Condensate and Feedwater Treatment]
    O --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram represents the **Mass-Burn Incinerator with Energy Recovery** layout. The mandatory **3Ts** (Temperature ≥ 850 °C, Time ≥ 2 s, Turbulence ≥ mixing Reynolds number) are implemented between blocks **C** and **D**.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Downdraft Gasifier

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Biomass or MSW Feed] --> Q[Hopper and Screw Feeder]
    Q --> R[Drying Zone 100 to 200 C]
    R --> S[Pyrolysis Zone 200 to 600 C]
    S --> T[Oxidation Zone 700 to 1200 C with Limited Air]
    T --> U[Reduction Zone 800 to 1000 C Char and CO2 H2O Reaction]
    U --> V[Producer Gas Outlet CO H2 CH4 N2]
    V --> W[Particulate Cyclone Separator]
    W --> X[Tar Condenser and Scrubber]
    X --> Y[Syngas Cooler and Filter]
    Y --> Z[Gas Holder and Buffer Storage]
    Z --> AA[Internal Combustion Engine Coupled to Generator]
    AA --> AB[Power Conditioning and Grid Tie]
    T --> AC[Ash Grate Discharge]
    AC --> AD[Ash Collection Bin]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four zones (Drying → Pyrolysis → Oxidation → Reduction) are **physically stacked vertically in a downdraft gasifier**. Air enters through tuyeres just above the grate, flows *downward* (co-current with fuel), and the syngas exits below the grate. This is the most common small-scale design.

## 4.3 Process Decision Flow — Combustion vs Gasification Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Waste Characterization] --> B0{Moisture above 40 percent}
    B0 -- Yes --> C0[Consider Drying First]
    C0 --> D0{Waste is Homogeneous Dry}
    B0 -- No --> D0
    D0 -- Yes --> E0[Use Downdraft Gasifier with IC Engine]
    D0 -- No --> F0{Is Daily Throughput above 500 tonnes}
    F0 -- Yes --> G0[Use Mass Burn Incinerator with Boiler]
    F0 -- No --> H0[Use Fluidized Bed Gasifier with Gas Cleanup]
    G0 --> I0[Need Electricity or Only Steam]
    I0 -- Electricity --> J0[Steam Turbine Cycle]
    I0 -- Steam --> K0[Process Heat Customer]
    E0 --> L0[Syngas for Engine and Drying]
    H0 --> M0[Syngas Cleaning and Pipeline or Engine]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This selection logic mirrors the **KTU Module 1 decision matrix** and is the expected answer structure for 14-mark design questions.

## 4.4 Energy and Mass Flow — Gasifier Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Dry Feedstock] --> MIX[Gasifier Reactor]
    IN2[Air or O2 in] --> MIX
    IN3[Steam Optional] --> MIX
    MIX --> OUT1[Syngas CO H2 CH4]
    MIX --> OUT2[Char and Tars]
    MIX --> OUT3[Bottom Ash]
    MIX -- Heat Out --> BOIL[Heat Recovery]
    OUT1 --> ENG[Engine or Boiler]
    ENG --> POW[Electrical Power]
    OUT2 --> RET[Reactor Recycle]
    RET --> MIX
    BOIL --> DH[District Heat or Drying]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define combustion with respect to waste utilization. State the "3Ts" that govern efficient combustion in an incinerator. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Combustion** is the rapid exothermic chemical reaction between the combustible fraction of waste (C, H, S) and oxygen, producing heat, CO₂, H₂O, and SO₂. It is the most widely practiced route for volume reduction and energy recovery from MSW.
>
> The **3Ts** of combustion, essential for complete burnout and dioxin destruction, are:
> 1. **Temperature** — Furnace temperature ≥ **850 °C** (1100 °C for chlorinated waste).
> 2. **Time** — Residence time of flue gases at high temperature ≥ **2 seconds**.
> 3. **Turbulence** — Adequate mixing of air and combustion gases for complete burnout, characterized by **Reynolds number > 10,000** in the combustion zone.
>
> Failure of any T causes **CO spikes, soot, and dioxin formation** — a common deduction in KTU valuation.

### Q2. Differentiate between combustion and gasification. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Combustion | Gasification |
|---|---|---|
| Oxygen supply | Excess / stoichiometric (ER > 1) | Sub-stoichiometric (ER 0.2–0.4) |
| Main product | Heat (flue gas) | **Syngas** (CO + H₂) |
| Temperature | 850–1200 °C | 600–900 °C |
| Residue | Ash | Ash + char + tars |
| Air-fuel ratio (MSW) | 5–8 | 1–2 |
| Use | Steam, electricity | Engine fuel, chemical feedstock |

> **One-line answer (fall-back):** Combustion is **fuel → heat**; Gasification is **fuel → gaseous fuel**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

> A municipal solid waste sample has the following ultimate analysis (by mass, dry basis):  
> C = 38 %, H = 5 %, S = 1.5 %, O = 30 %, N = 1.5 %, Ash = 24 %.  
> Moisture (as received) = 40 %.  
> **(a)** Calculate the stoichiometric oxygen requirement, theoretical air, and actual air for 40 % excess air. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Determine the HHV and LHV (as received) using Dulong's formula. Comment on the suitability of this waste for thermal treatment. **(7 marks)**

### Solution A:

#### Part (a) — Stoichiometric Air Calculation

**Step 1: Net O₂ required per kg dry waste (using KTU formula 4):**

$$
O_{2,\text{net}} = 2.667\,C + 8.0\,H + 1.0\,S - 1.0\,O
$$

$$
O_{2,\text{net}} = (2.667 \times 0.38) + (8.0 \times 0.05) + (1.0 \times 0.015) - (1.0 \times 0.30)
$$

$$
O_{2,\text{net}} = 1.0135 + 0.400 + 0.015 - 0.300 = 1.1285\ \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg dry fuel}
$$

**Step 2: Theoretical air required:**

$$
A_{\text{th}} = \frac{1.1285}{0.232} = 4.864\ \text{kg air / kg dry fuel}
$$

**Step 3: Actual air with 40 % excess:**

$$
A_{\text{act}} = 4.864 \times 1.40 = 6.810\ \text{kg air / kg dry fuel}
$$

**AFR (dry basis) = 6.81.** ✓ Within expected MSW range.

#### Part (b) — HHV, LHV, and Feasibility

**Step 4: HHV using Dulong's formula (KTU formula 9):**

$$
\text{HHV} = 0.338 \times 38 + 1.428 \times 5 + 0.095 \times 1.5 - 0.108 \times 30
$$

$$
\text{HHV} = 12.844 + 7.140 + 0.1425 - 3.240 = 16.886\ \text{MJ/kg dry basis}
$$

**Step 5: As-received HHV (after 40 % moisture):**

$$
\text{HHV}_{\text{ar}} = 16.886 \times (1 - 0.40) = 10.132\ \text{MJ/kg}
$$

**Step 6: LHV (dry basis, KTU formula 10):**

$$
\text{LHV}_{\text{dry}} = 16.886 - 9 \times 0.05 \times 2.442 = 16.886 - 1.099 = 15.787\ \text{MJ/kg}
$$

**Step 7: As-received LHV:**

$$
\text{LHV}_{\text{ar}} = 15.787 \times 0.60 = 9.472\ \text{MJ/kg}
$$

> **Suitability comment:** With LHV ≈ 9.5 MJ/kg, this is **just above the 7 MJ/kg threshold** for self-sustaining combustion (often cited as 6–8 MJ/kg). Auxiliary fuel (diesel/LPG) may be needed during start-up and for high-moisture feed variations. Gasification (or co-firing with dry RDF) is recommended for the wet-monsoon season typical of Kerala.

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four reaction zones of a downdraft gasifier**. How is the equivalence ratio (ER) used to distinguish gasification from combustion? **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** A 100 kg/h downdraft gasifier is fed with rice husk having HHV = 16 MJ/kg and moisture = 8 %. The dry producer gas has a flow rate of 220 Nm³/h with LHV = 5.0 MJ/Nm³. Calculate **(i)** the cold-gas efficiency and **(ii)** the carbon conversion efficiency, given that carbon in the syngas is 18 % by mass. **(7 marks)**

### Solution B:

#### Part (a) — Four Zones of Downdraft Gasifier

**Zone 1: Drying (100–200 °C)** — moisture evaporation; latent heat ≈ 2.26 MJ/kg water removed.  
**Zone 2: Pyrolysis (200–600 °C)** — volatiles released; char (fixed C) remains; produces tars and light HC gases.  
**Zone 3: Oxidation (700–1200 °C)** — exothermic char combustion with limited O₂; ER ≈ 0.25–0.30; releases heat to drive the reduction zone.  
**Zone 4: Reduction (800–1000 °C)** — endothermic:  
- $\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}$ (Boudouard)  
- $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$ (water-gas)  
- $\text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4$

**ER distinguishes processes:**
- $\text{ER} = 1$ → stoichiometric combustion
- $\text{ER} > 1$ → excess-air combustion
- $0.2 \le \text{ER} < 0.4$ → **gasification** (oxygen-starved)
- $\text{ER} = 0$ → pure pyrolysis

#### Part (b) — Efficiency Calculations

**Given:** $\dot{m}_f = 100\ \text{kg/h}$, HHV = 16 MJ/kg, moisture = 8 %, $\dot{V}_g = 220\ \text{Nm}^3/\text{h}$, LHV = 5.0 MJ/Nm³, %C in syngas = 18.

**Step 1: Energy input to the gasifier (using dry-basis HHV for technical efficiency):**

Dry fuel feed = $100 \times (1 - 0.08) = 92$ kg/h  
Energy input = $92 \times 16 = 1472$ MJ/h

**Step 2: Energy output in syngas (cold-gas basis):**

Energy output = $220 \times 5.0 = 1100$ MJ/h

**Step 3: Cold-gas efficiency (KTU formula 12):**

$$
\eta_{cg} = \frac{1100}{1472} = 0.7473 = 74.7\ \%
$$

> **Engineering comment:** 70–80 % cold-gas efficiency is *excellent* for a downdraft gasifier; it indicates low tar and high char conversion.

**Step 4: Carbon conversion efficiency:**

We need the carbon in the dry fuel. Assume typical rice husk contains ~38 % carbon (dry). Then:

$$
\text{C in feed} = 92 \times 0.38 = 34.96\ \text{kg C/h}
$$

Syngas mass flow ≈ $\dot{V}_g \times \rho_g \approx 220 \times 1.2\ \text{kg/Nm}^3 = 264\ \text{kg/h}$ (using average syngas density of 1.2 kg/Nm³).

$$
\text{C in syngas} = 264 \times 0.18 = 47.52\ \text{kg/h}
$$

Wait — this exceeds the C in feed, so our assumed density is too high (the syngas is lighter). Let us use a more realistic average density for hot raw producer gas (mostly N₂ and CO) of **1.15 kg/Nm³**:

$$
\text{Syngas mass} = 220 \times 1.15 = 253\ \text{kg/h}
$$

$$
\text{C in syngas} = 253 \times 0.18 = 45.5\ \text{kg/h}
$$

This still exceeds the C in feed. Therefore, the "18 % C in syngas" likely means **18 % by volume** (typical for syngas). **KTU expects you to be alert to unit confusion.**

**Correcting interpretation (18 % vol = mole basis, with syngas molar mass ≈ 24 g/mol):**

$$
\text{Syngas kmol/h} = 220 / 22.4 = 9.821\ \text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
\text{C in syngas (as C atoms)} = 0.18 \times 9.821 \times 12 = 21.21\ \text{kg C/h}
$$

$$
X_C = \frac{21.21}{34.96} = 0.607 = 60.7\ \%
$$

> **Interpretation:** ~61 % of the feed carbon is gasified. The remaining ~39 % stays as char (a saleable by-product in some designs) or is lost with bottom ash. A typical target is 70–90 %; this gasifier is operating at a *modest* carbon conversion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students almost always forget to subtract the **bound oxygen** in the O term of Dulong's formula. This loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> **Pitfall #2:** Reporting HHV instead of LHV, or forgetting to convert from dry to "as-received" basis. Another 1–2 marks lost.
> 
> **Pitfall #3:** Confusing mass% with vol% for syngas carbon content. KTU often mixes units to test the student's awareness.
> 
> **Pitfall #4:** Not writing units in the final answer. **Always carry units** — KTU explicitly awards 0.5–1 mark for "correct unit and significant figures."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

- **Combustion** = excess-air oxidation → heat; **Gasification** = sub-stoichiometric oxidation → syngas. The **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** is the *master switch* that distinguishes them.
- **Dulong's formula** (HHV = 0.338C + 1.428H + 0.095S − 0.108O in MJ/kg dry) is **the single most-asked formula** in this module — memorize it.
- The **3Ts** of combustion: **T**emperature ≥ 850 °C, **T**ime ≥ 2 s, **T**urbulence (Reynolds number high). These are the universal incinerator design constraints.
- The **four zones of a downdraft gasifier** are: **Drying → Pyrolysis → Oxidation → Reduction** (in vertical order, top to bottom).
- The **Boudouard reaction** (C + CO₂ → 2CO) and **water-gas reaction** (C + H₂O → CO + H₂) are the two most important *endothermic* reactions that produce syngas in the reduction zone.
- Always use **LHV for engine-based systems** (gasification) and **HHV for boiler/combustion systems** unless told otherwise. The **9H × 2.442** correction converts HHV to LHV.
- The mass fraction of O₂ in air is **0.232 (or 23.2 %)**. Do not use 0.21 (the volume fraction) in mass-based calculations.
- The **theoretical air** for typical MSW (C ≈ 40 %, H ≈ 5 %, O ≈ 25 %, ash ≈ 20 %) lies in the range **4.5–6 kg/kg dry fuel**; with 30–50 % excess air, the actual air is **6–8 kg/kg**.
- **High-moisture Kerala MSW** (>40 % moisture) has LHV below 7 MJ/kg and **cannot sustain combustion without auxiliary fuel or pre-drying**. This is a KTU favourite.
- A **cold-gas efficiency** of 70–80 % indicates a well-designed downdraft gasifier; a **carbon conversion** of 60–90 % is typical.
- **Common student mistakes to avoid:** (i) using the stoichiometric ratio 32/12 = 2.67 but forgetting to subtract the O term; (ii) computing air with volume fraction 21 %; (iii) omitting the LHV correction; (iv) reporting HHV on dry basis when the question asks for "as fired"; (v) not mentioning the 3Ts in combustion questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Extraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extraction** in waste and byproducts utilization is a **separation process** in which a desired soluble component (solute) is transferred from a **heterogeneous mixture** (solid or liquid waste/byproduct matrix) into a **selective solvent**, producing an **extract** (solvent-rich phase) and a **raffinate** (residual phase).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, extraction is broadly classified as:

- **Solid–Liquid Extraction (Leaching):** Solute is removed from a **solid waste/byproduct matrix** using a liquid solvent.
- **Liquid–Liquid Extraction (Solvent Extraction):** Solute is transferred between **two immiscible liquid phases**.
- **Mechanical Extraction (Expression):** Liquid/oil is pressed out of solid biological wastes (seeds, fruits) using physical force.
- **Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE):** Uses a solvent above its critical temperature and pressure (commonly CO₂) as the extracting medium.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Making Tea** ☕
> Imagine placing tea leaves (solid byproduct) in hot water (solvent). The soluble compounds (tannins, flavor, color) migrate from the solid into the water, producing a colored extract. The wet, spent leaves left behind are the **raffinate**. This is essentially **Solid–Liquid Extraction (Leaching)**.
>
> Similarly, when you wash oil-stained hands with soap (oil + water immiscible system), the dirt/oil transfers into the soap phase — this is **Liquid–Liquid Extraction**.

This analogy reinforces that **extraction is governed by solubility differences and mass transfer gradients** between phases.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Distribution Coefficient | $K_D$ | $0.1$ to $100$ |
| Separation Factor (Selectivity) | $\beta$ | $>1$ (preferably $>10$) |
| Extraction Yield / Recovery | $Y$ | $0$ to $100\,\%$ |
| Stage Efficiency | $\eta$ | $0.5$ to $0.95$ |
| Reflux Ratio (in staged extractors) | $R$ | $0.1$ to $5.0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **distribution coefficient** $K_D$ is the **most critical parameter** in extraction design and appears in almost every numerical question in KTU examinations. Standard units are **dimensionless** (concentration ratio at equilibrium).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principles of Extraction

The extraction process is governed by **three fundamental principles**:

1. **Solubility Difference** — The solute must be significantly more soluble in the extracting solvent than in the original matrix.
2. **Mass Transfer** — Driven by the **concentration gradient** between phases until thermodynamic equilibrium is reached.
3. **Phase Contact** — Adequate **interfacial area** and **contact time** must be ensured for efficient transfer.

---

## 2.2 Single-Stage Equilibrium Extraction

In a single-stage operation, the feed (mass $F$ with solute mass fraction $x_F$) is contacted once with a fresh solvent (mass $S$ with solute mass fraction $y_S$), reaching equilibrium. Two output streams are produced:

- **Extract** of mass $E$ (composition $y_E$)
- **Raffinate** of mass $R$ (composition $x_R$)

### Mass Balance Equations

**Overall Material Balance:**

$$F + S = R + E$$

**Solute Material Balance:**

$$F \cdot x_F + S \cdot y_S = R \cdot x_R + E \cdot y_E$$

**Equilibrium Relationship:**

$$y_E = K_D \cdot x_R$$

> [!NOTE]
> In **KTU Board Exams**, the standard assumption for fresh solvent is $y_S = 0$ unless explicitly stated otherwise.

---

## 2.3 Multi-Stage Extraction (Crosscurrent & Countercurrent)

### 2.3.1 Crosscurrent Extraction

Fresh solvent is added at **each stage** independently. Most effective for high recovery but solvent-intensive.

### 2.3.2 Countercurrent Extraction

The fresh solvent enters at the **final stage** and flows **opposite** to the feed direction. Most solvent-efficient for industrial applications.

The **Kremser Equation** (very high-yield for KTU numericals):

$$N = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\frac{x_F - \frac{y_S}{K_D}}{x_N - \frac{y_S}{K_D}}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{r}\right) + \frac{1}{r}\right]}{\ln r}$$

where $r = \frac{K_D \cdot S}{F}$ is the **extraction factor**, and $N$ is the **number of theoretical stages** required.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Distribution Coefficient | $K_D = \dfrac{y^*}{x}$ | Equilibrium relation between extract & raffinate |
| Selectivity (Separation Factor) | $\beta = \dfrac{K_D(A)}{K_D(B)}$ | Quantifies purity of separation |
| Extraction Yield | $Y = \dfrac{F x_F - R x_R}{F x_F} \times 100\,\%$ | Percentage of solute recovered |
| Extraction Factor | $r = \dfrac{K_D \cdot S}{F}$ | Used in Kremser equation |
| Kremser Equation | $N = \dfrac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_F - y_S/K_D}{x_N - y_S/K_D}\right)(1-1/r) + 1/r\right]}{\ln r}$ | Number of stages in countercurrent operation |
| Single-Stage Mass Balance | $F(x_F - x_R) = S(y_E - y_S)$ | Solute transfer equation |
| Mass Ratio (Solvent to Feed) | $S/F = \dfrac{x_F - x_R}{y_E - y_S}$ | Solvent requirement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Kremser Equation** is a **guaranteed high-weightage question** in KTU Module 1 numericals. Memorize the form of $r$ and the logarithmic structure.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Waste/Byproduct Source | Extraction Type | Product Recovered |
|---|---|---|
| Spent coffee grounds | Solid–liquid (Soxhlet) | Antioxidants, oils |
| Citrus peels | Mechanical pressing | D-Limonene, essential oils |
| Spent battery black mass | Hydrometallurgical leaching | Li, Co, Ni |
| Used cooking oil | Liquid–liquid | Biodiesel precursors |
| Rice bran | Solvent extraction | Rice bran oil |
| E-waste PCBs | Acid leaching + SX | Cu, Au, Pd |
| Bagasse | Supercritical CO₂ extraction | Bioactives, waxes |

---

## 2.6 Factors Affecting Extraction Efficiency

1. **Particle size** — Smaller particles increase interfacial area and improve leaching.
2. **Temperature** — Higher temperature increases solubility and diffusivity.
3. **Solvent-to-feed ratio** — Higher $S/F$ improves recovery.
4. **Agitation / Mixing** — Enhances mass transfer coefficient.
5. **pH** — Critical in hydrometallurgical extraction of metals.
6. **Number of stages** — More stages = higher recovery.
7. **Co-solute interference** — Reduces selectivity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Single-Stage Extraction Mass Balance

### Given
- Feed mass $F$ with solute mass fraction $x_F$
- Solvent mass $S$ with solute mass fraction $y_S = 0$ (fresh solvent)
- Distribution coefficient $K_D = \dfrac{y_E}{x_R}$

### Step 1 — Overall Material Balance

The total mass entering the extractor must equal the total mass leaving:

$$F + S = R + E \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$

### Step 2 — Solute Material Balance

Solute entering with both feed and solvent must equal solute leaving in extract and raffinate:

$$F \cdot x_F + S \cdot y_S = R \cdot x_R + E \cdot y_E \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$

Substituting $y_S = 0$:

$$F \cdot x_F = R \cdot x_R + E \cdot y_E \quad \text{...(Equation 2a)}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Equilibrium Relation

$$y_E = K_D \cdot x_R \quad \text{...(Equation 3)}$$

### Step 4 — Express Raffinate and Extract in terms of $x_R$

From Equation 1, solving for $E$:

$$E = F + S - R$$

Substituting into Equation 2a:

$$F x_F = R x_R + (F + S - R)(K_D x_R)$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $x_R$ (Final Composition of Raffinate)

Expanding:

$$F x_F = R x_R + (F + S) K_D x_R - R K_D x_R$$

$$F x_F = R x_R (1 - K_D) + (F + S) K_D x_R$$

$$F x_F = x_R \left[ R(1 - K_D) + (F + S) K_D \right]$$

Solving for $x_R$:

$$x_R = \frac{F \cdot x_F}{R(1 - K_D) + (F + S) K_D}$$

For the **common KTU case** where solvent and feed are immiscible (so $R \approx F$), this simplifies to:

$$x_R = \frac{x_F}{1 + \left(\dfrac{S}{F}\right) K_D} \quad \text{...(Final Result)}$$

### Step 6 — Fraction of Solute Remaining in Raffinate

$$\frac{x_R}{x_F} = \frac{1}{1 + (S/F) K_D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This final expression shows that increasing the **solvent-to-feed ratio** $S/F$ or the **distribution coefficient** $K_D$ reduces the fraction of solute remaining — i.e., **improves extraction efficiency**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:**
A solid byproduct contains $40\,\%$ oil by mass. It is contacted with $1000\,\text{kg}$ of fresh solvent (hexane) in a single-stage extractor. Feed mass = $500\,\text{kg}$. The equilibrium distribution coefficient $K_D = 2.5$ (kg solute/kg solvent ÷ kg solute/kg solid). Assume solvent and solid are immiscible. Find:
1. Mass of oil remaining in the raffinate.
2. Percentage extraction yield.

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Identify Values

$$F = 500\,\text{kg}, \quad x_F = 0.40, \quad S = 1000\,\text{kg}, \quad K_D = 2.5$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Derived Single-Stage Formula

$$x_R = \frac{x_F}{1 + (S/F) K_D}$$

$$x_R = \frac{0.40}{1 + \left(\dfrac{1000}{500}\right)(2.5)}$$

$$x_R = \frac{0.40}{1 + 2 \times 2.5} = \frac{0.40}{1 + 5} = \frac{0.40}{6}$$

$$x_R = 0.0667$$

### Step 3 — Mass of Oil in Raffinate

Since $R \approx F = 500\,\text{kg}$:

$$\text{Mass of oil remaining} = R \cdot x_R = 500 \times 0.0667 = 33.33\,\text{kg}$$

### Step 4 — Extraction Yield

$$Y = \frac{F x_F - R x_R}{F x_F} \times 100\,\%$$

$$Y = \frac{(500)(0.40) - (500)(0.0667)}{(500)(0.40)} \times 100\,\%$$

$$Y = \frac{200 - 33.33}{200} \times 100\,\%$$

$$Y = \frac{166.67}{200} \times 100\,\% = 83.33\,\%$$

### Final Answer

- Mass of oil remaining in raffinate = **$33.33\,\text{kg}$**
- Extraction yield = **$83.33\,\%$**

> [!NOTE]
> This type of problem is **standard 7-mark KTU Part B question**. Students must clearly identify $F$, $S$, $K_D$, and substitute into the derived single-stage formula.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — General Extraction Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed: Solid or Liquid Waste/Byproduct] --> B[Pre-treatment: Crushing, Drying, Sieving]
    B --> C[Mixing Stage: Solvent Addition]
    C --> D[Equilibrium Contact with Agitation]
    D --> E[Phase Separation: Settler / Centrifuge / Filter]
    E --> F[Raffinate: Spent Solid or Liquid Residue]
    E --> G[Extract: Solvent + Solute Mixture]
    G --> H[Solvent Recovery: Distillation or Evaporation]
    H --> I[Pure Extract / Recovered Product]
    H --> J[Recycled Solvent Returns to Mixing Stage]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Single-Stage vs Multi-Stage Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SS[Single Stage Extraction]
        A1[Feed F] --> M1[Mixer + Settler]
        S1[Fresh Solvent S] --> M1
        M1 --> R1[Raffinate R]
        M1 --> E1[Extract E]
    end

    subgraph CC[Countercurrent Multi-Stage Extraction]
        A2[Feed F enters Stage 1] --> ST1[Stage 1 Mixer-Settler]
        ST1 --> ST2[Stage 2 Mixer-Settler]
        ST2 --> ST3[Stage N Mixer-Settler]
        S2[Fresh Solvent S enters Stage N] --> ST3
        ST3 --> R2[Final Raffinate R_N]
        ST1 --> E2[Final Extract E_1]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Liquid–Liquid Extraction (Immisible Solvent System)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Aqueous Feed Phase] --> B[Mixer: Agitated Contact]
    C[Organic Solvent Phase] --> B
    B --> D[Settler / Decanter]
    D --> E[Raffinate: Aqueous Phase Depleted in Solute]
    D --> F[Extract: Organic Phase Enriched in Solute]
    F --> G[Distillation / Stripping]
    G --> H[Recovered Solute Product]
    G --> I[Recycled Solvent]
    I --> C
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CO2 Cylinder] --> B[Compressor + Heater]
    B --> C[Supercritical CO2: T>Tc, P>Pc]
    C --> D[Extraction Vessel Containing Solid Waste]
    D --> E[Expanded CO2 + Dissolved Solute]
    E --> F[Separator: Pressure Reduction]
    F --> G[Recovered Extract / Bioactive Compound]
    F --> H[Recycled CO2 Gas Returns to Compressor]
    H --> B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SFE (Supercritical Fluid Extraction)** is a **frequently asked conceptual question** in KTU exams because of its **green, solvent-free, recyclable** nature — making it highly relevant to **waste utilization** and **circular economy** themes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term **"Distribution Coefficient"** in the context of liquid–liquid extraction. How does it influence the design of an extraction process?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **distribution coefficient** $K_D$ is defined as the ratio of the **equilibrium concentration of the solute in the extract phase** to that in the **raffinate phase** at a given temperature:

$$K_D = \frac{y_E}{x_R}$$

A **higher $K_D$** indicates that the solute has a greater affinity for the solvent (extract phase), which results in:
- Higher extraction yield per stage
- Fewer stages required to achieve target recovery
- Lower solvent-to-feed ratio required

A $K_D$ value close to **1.0** is undesirable as it implies poor selectivity. For practical industrial extractions, $K_D > 1$ is preferred, and $K_D > 10$ is considered excellent.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Differentiate between **single-stage** and **multi-stage crosscurrent** extraction with respect to solvent consumption and recovery.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Single-Stage | Multi-Stage Crosscurrent |
|---|---|---|
| Solvent addition | Once (single batch) | Fresh solvent added at each stage |
| Recovery | Lower | Higher (closer to 100%) |
| Solvent consumption | Lower total | Higher total |
| Equipment complexity | Simple (1 mixer-settler) | Complex (multiple units) |
| Use case | Crude separation, low-value | High-purity recovery |

In **single-stage extraction**, the solvent is contacted with feed only once; equilibrium limits recovery. In **multi-stage crosscurrent**, fresh solvent at each stage maintains a high driving concentration gradient, enabling near-complete recovery — at the cost of greater solvent usage.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, the following questions offer **internal choice**. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of **Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE)** with a neat block diagram. Discuss why **supercritical CO₂** is the most preferred solvent for SFE in waste/byproduct recovery. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solid waste containing $30\,\%$ (by mass) of a valuable organic compound is to be extracted using an organic solvent. The feed mass is $800\,\text{kg}$ and solvent mass used is $1200\,\text{kg}$. The distribution coefficient $K_D = 3.0$. Assuming fresh solvent and immiscible phases, calculate the **single-stage extraction efficiency** and the **mass of compound remaining in the raffinate**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Working Principle of SFE:**

In SFE, a solvent (typically CO₂) is raised above its **critical temperature** ($T_c = 31.1\,°\text{C}$) and **critical pressure** ($P_c = 73.8\,\text{bar}$). Above these values, the solvent exhibits **liquid-like density** (good solvating power) and **gas-like diffusivity** (fast penetration into solid matrices).

**Process Steps:**
1. CO₂ is compressed and heated to supercritical state.
2. Supercritical CO₂ flows through the extraction vessel containing the solid waste.
3. The target solute dissolves into the supercritical fluid.
4. The extract-laden CO₂ is then passed through a **separator** where pressure is reduced, causing CO₂ to revert to gas phase and **precipitating the solute**.
5. CO₂ gas is recycled.

**Why Supercritical CO₂?**

- **Non-toxic** and **environmentally benign**
- **Tunable solvating power** by adjusting pressure/temperature
- **Easy separation** from extract (no solvent residue)
- **Low critical temperature** prevents thermal degradation of bioactive compounds
- **Recyclable** → aligns with **green chemistry** and **circular economy** principles
- **GRAS (Generally Recognized as Safe)** by FDA — suitable for food/pharma waste valorization

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- $F = 800\,\text{kg}$, $x_F = 0.30$, $S = 1200\,\text{kg}$, $K_D = 3.0$, $y_S = 0$

**Step 1 — Apply Single-Stage Formula**

$$x_R = \frac{x_F}{1 + (S/F) K_D}$$

$$x_R = \frac{0.30}{1 + \left(\dfrac{1200}{800}\right)(3.0)} = \frac{0.30}{1 + 1.5 \times 3.0}$$

$$x_R = \frac{0.30}{1 + 4.5} = \frac{0.30}{5.5} = 0.0545$$

**Step 2 — Mass of Compound in Raffinate**

$$\text{Mass} = R \cdot x_R = 800 \times 0.0545 = 43.64\,\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Extraction Efficiency (Yield)**

$$Y = \frac{F x_F - R x_R}{F x_F} \times 100\,\% = \frac{240 - 43.64}{240} \times 100\,\%$$

$$Y = \frac{196.36}{240} \times 100\,\% = 81.82\,\%$$

**Final Answer:**
- Mass of compound remaining in raffinate = **$43.64\,\text{kg}$**
- Single-stage extraction efficiency = **$81.82\,\%$**

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of a flow diagram, explain **liquid–liquid extraction** for recovery of metal ions from e-waste leachate. Discuss the role of **selectivity (separation factor)** in choosing the extractant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **single-stage mass balance equation** for an extraction process where the feed $F = 600\,\text{kg}$, solvent $S = 900\,\text{kg}$, $x_F = 0.25$, and $K_D = 2.0$. Determine the **number of ideal stages** required to achieve $95\,\%$ solute recovery if the same fresh solvent quantity is used per stage in **crosscurrent** mode. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Liquid–Liquid Extraction of Metals from E-Waste:**

E-waste (PCBs, batteries) is first **leached** using acids (H₂SO₄, HCl, aqua regia) to dissolve target metals (Cu, Ni, Co, Li) into aqueous solution. This leachate is then contacted with an **organic phase** containing a selective **extractant** (e.g., D2EHPA, Cyanex 272, LIX 84-I).

**Process Flow:**
1. **Leaching** of e-waste with acid → metal-rich aqueous phase.
2. **Mixing** of aqueous and organic phases in a mixer → solute transfers to organic phase.
3. **Phase separation** in settler → organic extract and aqueous raffinate.
4. **Stripping** of loaded organic → recovery of pure metal.
5. **Solvent regeneration** and recycle.

**Role of Selectivity (Separation Factor) $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \frac{K_D(\text{Metal A})}{K_D(\text{Metal B})}$$

A high $\beta$ (typically $> 10$) ensures that the chosen extractant preferentially extracts **Metal A** over **Metal B**. This is critical for separating **mixtures of metals** (e.g., Co from Ni) and achieving high product purity. **Selective extractants** reduce the number of purification stages downstream.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- $F = 600\,\text{kg}$, $S = 900\,\text{kg}$ (per stage), $x_F = 0.25$, $K_D = 2.0$

**Step 1 — Derive Single-Stage Equation**

From the standard derivation (Section 3.1):

$$x_R = \frac{x_F}{1 + (S/F) K_D}$$

For our values:

$$\frac{x_R}{x_F} = \frac{1}{1 + (900/600)(2.0)} = \frac{1}{1 + 3.0} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

So **25%** of the solute remains after **each** crosscurrent stage.

**Step 2 — Recovery Required**

For $95\,\%$ recovery, residual fraction $= 0.05$:

$$\left(\frac{x_R}{x_F}\right)^N = 0.05$$

$$(0.25)^N = 0.05$$

**Step 3 — Solve for N (number of stages)**

Taking natural logarithm:

$$N \ln(0.25) = \ln(0.05)$$

$$N = \frac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.25)} = \frac{-2.9957}{-1.3863} \approx 2.16$$

Since stages must be a whole number, $N = 3$ stages are required.

**Step 4 — Verification with $N = 3$:**

$$\left(\frac{x_R}{x_F}\right)^3 = (0.25)^3 = 0.015625 \approx 1.56\,\% \text{ residual}$$

Recovery $= 98.44\,\% > 95\,\%$ ✓

**Final Answer:** **3 ideal stages** are required in crosscurrent mode.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting $y_S = 0$ assumption** — Fresh solvent carries no solute. Many students wrongly include $y_S$ in mass balance when problem states "fresh solvent."
> 2. **Mixing up $K_D$ definition** — Some students write $K_D = x_R / y_E$ instead of $y_E / x_R$. This reverses all subsequent calculations.
> 3. **Not clearly stating assumptions** (immiscible phases, single-stage equilibrium, constant $K_D$). Examiners award marks for explicit assumption statements.
> 4. **Crosscurrent vs Countercurrent confusion** — In crosscurrent, $S/F$ ratio and $K_D$ are used per stage; in countercurrent, the **Kremser equation** is mandatory.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing kg and g, or wt% and mass fraction, leads to numerical errors worth 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Skipping derivation steps** — Always show substitution of equations; partial marks are awarded for each correct algebraic step.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Extraction** is a separation process that exploits **solubility differences** and **mass transfer** between phases.
- ✅ Two primary types: **Solid–Liquid (Leaching)** and **Liquid–Liquid (Solvent Extraction)**.
- ✅ **Distribution Coefficient** $K_D = y_E / x_R$ is the **single most important parameter** in extraction design.
- ✅ For a single-stage extractor with fresh solvent, the **mass balance** simplifies to:

$$x_R = \frac{x_F}{1 + (S/F) K_D}$$

- ✅ **Extraction Yield** = $\dfrac{F x_F - R x_R}{F x_F} \times 100\,\%$
- ✅ **Crosscurrent** extraction uses fresh solvent per stage — simple but solvent-intensive.
- ✅ **Countercurrent** extraction is more solvent-efficient; uses the **Kremser Equation**:

$$N = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_F - y_S/K_D}{x_N - y_S/K_D}\right)(1-1/r) + 1/r\right]}{\ln r}$$

- ✅ **Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE)** uses CO₂ above its critical point ($T_c = 31.1\,°\text{C}$, $P_c = 73.8\,\text{bar}$) — **green, recyclable, no residue**.
- ✅ Key factors: particle size, temperature, $S/F$ ratio, agitation, pH, number of stages.
- ✅ **Selectivity / Separation Factor** $\beta = K_D(A) / K_D(B)$ governs purity in multi-component extraction.
- ✅ Always state assumptions: **immiscible phases, equilibrium at stage exit, fresh solvent ($y_S = 0$).**
- ✅ Real-world applications: oil from seeds, metals from e-waste, antioxidants from food waste, bioactive compounds from agro-residues.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anaerobic digestion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Anaerobic digestion # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** is a biochemical process in which biodegradable organic matter is broken down by a consortium of **microorganisms in the strict absence of molecular oxygen (O₂)**, producing a gaseous mixture called **biogas** (primarily CH₄ and CO₂) and a nutrient-rich slurry called **digestate**. The overall generalized stoichiometric reaction for a generic carbohydrate substrate is represented as: $$C_6H_{10}O_5 + H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{anaerobic bacteria}} 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2$$ The standard methane yield from carbohydrates is theoretically **0.415 m³ CH₄ / kg VS destroyed**, while for fats and proteins it ranges from **0.6 to 1.0 m³ CH₄ / kg VS destroyed**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of Anaerobic Digestion as "Decomposition in a Sealed Lunch Box" 🍱** Imagine you seal wet kitchen waste inside an airtight steel container and leave it untouched for weeks. Without air (oxygen), a silent, invisible army of bacteria begins dismantling the food.
