## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
> **Define briquetted fuel. List any four agro-residues suitable for briquetting.**

**Model Answer:**
Briquetted fuel is a densified solid biofuel produced by applying high mechanical pressure on loose biomass such as agricultural and forestry residues, without the addition of binders, to obtain uniform-shaped compact blocks with high bulk density and improved combustion properties.

Four suitable agro-residues:
1. **Rice husk** — abundant in India, ~22 MT/year production
2. **Sawdust** — byproduct of timber industry, ~15 MT/year
3. **Groundnut shell** — hard shell with high calorific value
4. **Coffee husk** — abundant in Karnataka and Kerala

*(Note: Any four relevant residues are acceptable)*

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
> **What is densification ratio? How is it calculated for a biomass briquette?**

**Model Answer:**
The **densification ratio** is the ratio of the density of the briquette to the density of the loose biomass before compaction. It indicates the degree of compaction achieved during briquetting.

$$\eta_d = \dfrac{\rho_{briquette}}{\rho_{loose}}$$

**Example:** If loose rice husk density = 110 kg/m³ and briquette density = 1,100 kg/m³, then:
$$\eta_d = 1{,}100 / 110 = 10$$

This means the briquette is **10 times denser** than the loose biomass.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 - Model Paper Pattern - Module 2 Internal Choice]**

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1

> **Q.A.(a)** **[7 Marks]** Explain the complete process of manufacturing briquetted fuel from rice husk with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the role of moisture content and particle size in determining briquette quality.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction [1 Mark]:**
Briquetting is the process of densifying loose biomass (bulk density 100–200 kg/m³) into compact solid blocks (density 600–1,200 kg/m³) by applying mechanical pressure, thereby improving its combustion, storage, and transportation properties.

**Process Steps [4 Marks]:**

1. **Collection and Sorting:** Rice husk is collected from rice mills, cleaned to remove stones, metals, and foreign matter.

2. **Drying:** Fresh rice husk has moisture content of 60–70%. It is dried in rotary drum dryers or by sun-drying to reduce moisture to **8–12%** (critical for binding and combustion).

3. **Grinding:** The dried husk is passed through a hammer mill to achieve particle size of **2–6 mm**. Fine particles bind better under pressure.

4. **Briquetting (Compaction):** The ground husk is fed into a screw/piston press. Pressure of **1,000–3,000 kg/cm²** is applied. Frictional heat (200–300°C) softens the **lignin**, which acts as a natural binder, fusing the particles together.

5. **Cooling and Curing:** Briquettes are cooled for 30–60 minutes. As lignin re-solidifies, the briquette hardens and gains mechanical strength.

6. **Packaging:** Cooled briquettes are packed in HDPE bags (25–50 kg units) for transport.

**Role of Moisture [1 Mark]:**
- Below 8%: Insufficient moisture → brittle briquettes, poor binding
- 8–12%: Optimal range → strong, durable briquettes
- Above 15%: Excess moisture → steam explosion during compaction, cracks, low density

**Role of Particle Size [1 Mark]:**
- < 2 mm: Fine dust, slow production rate
- 2–6 mm: Optimal — good interlocking and binding
- > 6 mm: Coarse particles — poor binding, weak briquettes

---

> **Q.A.(b)** **[7 Marks]** With the help of a formula, derive the calorific value of a sawdust briquette containing 12% moisture. Compare its heating value with that of bituminous coal. Discuss the environmental advantages of briquetted fuel.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- CV (dry basis) of sawdust = 4,500 kcal/kg
- Moisture content (wet basis) = 12%
- Latent heat of vaporization $L_v$ = 540 kcal/kg

**Step 1: Apply moisture correction formula**

$$CV_{effective} = CV_{dry} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{MC}{100}\right) - L_v \times \dfrac{MC}{100}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$CV_{effective} = 4{,}500 \times 0.88 - 540 \times 0.12$$

$$CV_{effective} = 3{,}960 - 64.8$$

$$\boxed{CV_{effective} = 3{,}895.2 \text{ kcal/kg}}$$

**Step 3: Comparison with Bituminous Coal (5,500–7,000 kcal/kg) [2 Marks]**

| Parameter | Sawdust Briquette | Bituminous Coal |
|---|---|---|
| Calorific Value | 3,895 kcal/kg | 6,000 kcal/kg (avg.) |
| Ash | 1–3% | 10–15% |
| Sulfur | < 0.1% | 0.5–3.0% |

**Substitution Ratio:** Approximately **1.5–1.8 tonnes** of sawdust briquettes substitute 1 tonne of bituminous coal.

**Environmental Advantages [2 Marks]:**

1. **Carbon Neutral:** CO₂ released during combustion equals CO₂ absorbed during plant growth → closed carbon cycle.

2. **Low Sulfur Emissions:** < 0.1% S content prevents SO₂ pollution and acid rain.

3. **Waste Utilization:** Converts agricultural waste (which would otherwise be burned in fields causing pollution) into a useful fuel.

4. **Reduced Deforestation:** Reduces demand for firewood, preserving forest cover.

5. **Lower NOx:** Combustion temperatures are lower than coal, reducing NOx formation.

6. **Ash Utilization:** Briquette ash can be used as soil conditioner (potash-rich) or in cement manufacture.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Internal Choice)

> **Q.B.(a)** **[7 Marks]** Describe the different types of briquetting machines. Compare piston press and screw press briquetting systems with respect to pressure, capacity, energy consumption, and product quality.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction [1 Mark]:**
Briquetting machines convert loose biomass into densified solid fuel. They differ in mechanism of pressure application, capacity, and suitability for different feedstocks.

**Types of Briquetting Machines [3 Marks]:**

1. **Piston Press (Mechanical/Hydraulic):**
   - A piston compresses biomass in a cylindrical die
   - Produces hollow-centered briquettes (needles)
   - Suitable for: rice husk, sawdust, soft biomass
   - Pressure: 800–1,500 kg/cm²

2. **Screw Press (Extruder):**
   - Biomass is pushed through a tapered die by a rotating screw
   - Produces solid, dense, cylindrical briquettes
   - Suitable for: hard biomass (shells, husks)
   - Pressure: 2,000–4,000 kg/cm²

3. **Roller Press (Compactor):**
   - Biomass forced between two counter-rotating rollers
   - High capacity industrial application
   - Pressure: 1,500–3,000 kg/cm²

4. **Pellet Mill:**
   - Small diameter (6–12 mm) pellets
   - Used in feed industry and for domestic stoves
   - Pressure: 500–1,000 kg/cm²

**Comparison: Piston vs Screw Press [3 Marks]:**

| Parameter | Piston Press | Screw Press |
|---|---|---|
| **Pressure** | 800–1,500 kg/cm² | 2,000–4,000 kg/cm² |
| **Capacity** | 100–500 kg/hr | 80–300 kg/hr |
| **Energy Consumption** | Lower (15–25 kWh/tonne) | Higher (40–70 kWh/tonne) |
| **Briquette Density** | 600–900 kg/m³ | 900–1,400 kg/m³ |
| **Product Quality** | Hollow center, variable | Solid, uniform, durable |
| **Wear & Tear** | Moderate | High (screw erosion) |
| **Best For** | Soft biomass, low cost | Hard biomass, premium quality |
| **Capital Cost** | Lower | Higher |

---

> **Q.B.(b)** **[7 Marks]** A rice husk sample of 100 kg is collected with a moisture content of 65% (wet basis). After sun-drying, its weight reduces to 38 kg. The dried husk is briquetted into blocks of density 1,000 kg/m³. Calculate: (i) Final moisture content after drying, (ii) Energy density of briquette (CV = 3,500 kcal/kg on dry basis), and (iii) Compression ratio if the loose husk density is 120 kg/m³.

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Initial wet mass $M_{w1}$ = 100 kg
- Initial MC (wet basis) = 65%
- Mass after drying $M_{w2}$ = 38 kg (this is the new wet mass with reduced moisture)
- Briquette density $\rho_b$ = 1,000 kg/m³
- Calorific value (dry basis) $CV_{dry}$ = 3,500 kcal/kg
- Loose husk density $\rho_{loose}$ = 120 kg/m³
- Latent heat $L_v$ = 540 kcal/kg

**Step (i): Final Moisture Content (Wet Basis) [2 Marks]**

The dry mass remains constant during drying:
$$M_d = M_{w1} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{MC_1}{100}\right) = 100 \times (1 - 0.65) = 35 \text{ kg}$$

After drying:
$$MC_2 = \dfrac{M_{w2} - M_d}{M_{w2}} \times 100 = \dfrac{38 - 35}{38} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{MC_2 = 7.89\%}$$

> **Interpretation:** The moisture has dropped from 65% to 7.89%, which is **within the optimal briquetting range of 8–12%** (slightly below optimum — usable).

**Step (ii): Energy Density of Briquette [3 Marks]**

**Effective CV after moisture correction:**
$$CV_{eff} = CV_{dry} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{MC_2}{100}\right) - L_v \times \dfrac{MC_2}{100}$$

$$CV_{eff} = 3{,}500 \times (1 - 0.0789) - 540 \times 0.0789$$

$$CV_{eff} = 3{,}500 \times 0.9211 - 42.6$$

$$CV_{eff} = 3{,}223.85 - 42.6$$

$$CV_{eff} = 3{,}181.25 \text{ kcal/kg}$$

**Energy Density:**
$$ED = \rho_b \times CV_{eff} = 1{,}000 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 3{,}181.25 \text{ kcal/kg}$$

$$\boxed{ED = 3{,}181{,}250 \text{ kcal/m}^3 = 3{,}181.25 \text{ Mcal/m}^3}$$

**Step (iii): Compression Ratio [2 Marks]**

$$CR = \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_{loose}} = \dfrac{1{,}000}{120}$$

$$\boxed{CR = 8.33}$$

> **Interpretation:** A compression ratio of 8.33 falls **within the optimal range of 5–12**, indicating good quality briquetting with efficient volume reduction.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PGAET753 Module 2 (Briquetting):**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion (Loss: 1–2 marks):** Always write units in formulas. The densification ratio is dimensionless, but density has units of kg/m³. CV is in kcal/kg. Mixing them up is a common error.
>
> 2. **Moisture Basis Confusion (Loss: 1 mark):** When the question says "moisture content," **always specify whether it is wet basis or dry basis**. Default in engineering is wet basis unless specified.
>
> 3. **Skipping Latent Heat Term (Loss: 1–2 marks):** When calculating effective calorific value of moist briquettes, many students forget the latent heat of vaporization ($L_v = 540$ kcal/kg). Examiner deducts marks for this.
>
> 4. **Confusing Calorific Values (Loss: 1 mark):** Remember that briquette CV is **lower** than dry biomass CV due to moisture. Do not quote coal CV values for briquettes.
>
> 5. **Compression vs Densification Ratio (Loss: 1 mark):** Compression Ratio = Volume ratio; Densification Ratio = Density ratio. They are reciprocal in some texts but defined as per the formula table provided above.
>
> 6. **Diagram Without Labels (Loss: 2 marks):** Flow diagrams without labeled process stages (Collection → Drying → Grinding → Briquetting → Cooling) receive **partial credit only**. Always label all process units.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU PGAET753 — Module 2: Briquetted Fuel — Rapid Revision Checklist**

### Core Concepts
- Briquetted fuel = densified biomass (compressed agro-residue blocks)
- Primary feedstocks: **Rice husk, Groundnut shell, Coffee husk, Sawdust, Coconut shell**
- Densification ratio $\eta_d$ = briquette density ÷ loose density (typically 5–12)
- Energy density is the **most important engineering metric** (kcal/m³ or MJ/m³)

### Process Sequence (Memorize Order)
**Collection → Sorting → Drying → Grinding → Compaction → Cooling → Packaging**

### Critical Parameters
- **Moisture content:** 8–12% (optimum), below 8% → brittle, above 15% → cracked
- **Particle size:** 2–6 mm (optimum)
- **Pressure:** 1,000–3,000 kg/cm²
- **Temperature rise:** 200–300°C (activates lignin binder)

### Standard Reference Values (Memorize)
- Bulk density of loose husk: **100–200 kg/m³**
- Bulk density of briquette: **600–1,200 kg/m³**
- Calorific value of briquette: **3,500–5,000 kcal/kg**
- Latent heat of water: **540 kcal/kg**
- Rice husk ash content: **17–22%** (very high due to silica)

### Key Formula Triad (Always Remember)
1. $\eta_d = \rho_{briquette} / \rho_{loose}$
2. $CV_{eff} = CV_{dry} \times (1 - MC/100) - L_v \times MC/100$
3. $ED = \rho_b \times CV_{eff}$

### Machines & Applications
- **Piston Press:** Soft biomass, low cost, hollow briquettes
- **Screw Press:** Hard biomass, high density, high energy input
- **Roller Press:** Industrial scale, high capacity
- **Pellet Mill:** Small diameter (6–12 mm), domestic use

### Environmental Edge
- **Carbon neutral:** Briquettes do not add net CO₂ to atmosphere
- **Low sulfur (< 0.1%):** Reduces acid rain
- **Waste to wealth:** Converts agricultural residue into commercial fuel
- **Rural employment:** Decentralized briquetting plants generate local jobs

### One-Line Exam Punchlines
- "Briquetted fuel is **densified sunlight** stored in agro-residue."
- "**Lignin is nature's binder** — activated by friction heat during briquetting."
- "1 tonne of rice husk briquette replaces ~0.4 tonnes of furnace oil."
- "Briquettes are **carbon neutral** but not **carbon-free**."
- "**MNRE** promotes biomass briquetting under the National Biomass Cookstoves Programme."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection and design of briquetting machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Selection and design of briquetting machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Briquetting** is a *mechanical densification process* in which loose, low-bulk-density particulate residues (biomass, agricultural waste, industrial by-products, MSW fractions) are compacted under controlled pressure—often with the simultaneous application of heat and a binder (or by exploiting the natural lignin of the feedstock)—into a uniformly shaped, high-density solid fuel or product known as a **briquette**.

The **selection and design of briquetting machines** is the engineering discipline of matching machine architecture, die geometry, applied pressure, throughput requirement, and energy budget to the physical, chemical, and rheological characteristics of a given waste/byproduct stream. It is governed by feedstock properties (moisture, particle size, lignin/cellulose content, abrasive index) and the desired end-product specifications (density, shape, durability, water-resistance, calorific value).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor:** The selection-design problem is *not* a single calculation—it is a *multi-criteria decision matrix* where the student must justify machine type (piston / screw / roller / hydraulic / pneumatic) using feedstock properties, then size the screw/die/roller using geometric and power equations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Toothpaste Tube" Mental Model

Imagine squeezing a half-empty toothpaste tube with your thumb. The empty, floppy tube represents **loose biomass** (low bulk density ~50–200 kg/m³). As you compress it, the material is forced through a narrow nozzle—the *die*—and emerges as a continuous, dense, structurally stable cylinder. The harder you push, the denser the output. The *tube wall* is the *barrel*, your *thumb* is the *ram or screw*, and the *nozzle* is the *die*.

Now imagine three different squeezing mechanisms:
- **Your thumb (back-and-forth)** → **Piston / Ram Press** (intermittent output)
- **A slow-turning screw pushing paste forward** → **Screw Press / Extruder** (continuous output, high compression)
- **Two spinning rollers crushing paste between them with molded pockets** → **Roller Press** (continuous, no binder usually needed)

This single analogy unifies the three principal industrial briquetting architectures.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Selection AND Design"?**
> - **Selection** = Choosing the *type* of machine (qualitative decision matrix)
> - **Design** = Calculating the *geometric & power* parameters (quantitative)
> KTU questions typically split into:
> - 3-mark: Comparative selection (ram vs. screw vs. roller)
> - 7-mark: Die geometry / Compression ratio derivation
> - 7-mark: Power/throughput sizing of a screw press

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics Used

- **Specific pressure:** 1000–2500 kg/cm² for biomass briquettes
- **Bulk density of feed:** 60–200 kg/m³ (loose agro-residue)
- **Briquette density:** 800–1200 kg/m³
- **Compression ratio (CR):** 5–7 for most biomass feeds
- **Moisture content optimum:** 8–12% (wet basis)
- **Particle size optimum:** < 6 mm
- **L/D ratio of die (screw press):** 4–7

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Viva:** "**S**election = **S**ubjective (feedstock) and **D**esign = **D**eterministic (equations)."
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Selection Criteria — The Multi-Parameter Decision Matrix

The selection of a briquetting machine is dictated by the following engineering parameters, evaluated holistically:

### A. Feedstock Characteristics
- **Moisture content (MC % wb):**
  - MC < 8% → Binder usually required (screw press preferred)
  - MC = 8–12% → Optimum for most biomass (no binder needed for screw)
  - MC > 15% → Pre-drying mandatory; piston press tolerant
- **Lignin content:** > 20% → Self-binding under heat/pressure (screw extruder works naturally)
- **Fibrousness / Aspect ratio:** Long fibers (rice straw, bagasse) → Piston press better
- **Abrasive index (silica content):** High → Hardened dies (WC-coated), lower screw speed
- **Ash content:** > 10% → Roller press preferred (screw extruder wears fast)

### B. Production Capacity
- < 100 kg/hr → Pneumatic / manual piston
- 100–500 kg/hr → Mechanical piston / small screw
- 500–2000 kg/hr → Industrial screw extruder
- > 2000 kg/hr → Roller press / multi-screw

### C. Product Specification
- Pillow / ovoid shape → Roller press with hemispherical pockets
- Cylindrical / hexagonal log → Screw press with multi-hole die
- Briquette density requirement → Determines die L/D

## 2.2 The Three Principal Machine Architectures

| Parameter | Piston (Ram) Press | Screw Press (Extruder) | Roller Press |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Operating principle** | Reciprocating | Continuous, tapered screw | Two counter-rotating rollers |
| **Specific pressure** | 500–1500 kg/cm² | 1000–2500 kg/cm² | 200–600 kg/cm² |
| **Output shape** | Logs, pillows | Cylinders, hexagons | Pillow, ovoid, spherical |
| **Throughput** | 50–300 kg/hr | 200–2500 kg/hr | 500–5000 kg/hr |
| **Energy consumption** | 30–60 kWh/ton | 40–80 kWh/ton | 20–40 kWh/ton |
| **Heating** | External | Frictional (self-heating) | External (optional) |
| **Binder needed** | Usually yes | Usually no (lignin activation) | Sometimes |
| **Wear** | Low | High (screw & die) | Medium (roller shells) |
| **Best for** | Sawdust, rice husk, charcoal | Sawdust, groundnut shell, bagasse | Coal, coke, mineral fines |

## 2.3 Design Parameters — The "Six Pillars" of Briquetting Machine Design

1. **Compression Ratio (CR)** — Governs densification extent
2. **Die L/D ratio** — Governs extrusion back-pressure
3. **Specific pressure (pₛ)** — Governs lignin plasticization
4. **Screw geometry** — Pitch, flight depth, taper angle
5. **Power & torque** — Governs motor sizing
6. **Throughput equation** — Governs capacity sizing

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **directly tested** in KTU ESE Module-2 questions. Memorize the units.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{1. Compression Ratio (CR)} \quad && CR \;=\; \dfrac{L_f}{L_b} \;=\; \dfrac{V_f}{V_b} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_f} \quad \text{(dimensionless)} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{2. Specific Pressure} \quad && p_s \;=\; \dfrac{F}{A} \quad \text{[N/m² or kg/cm²]} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{3. Power Required (screw shaft)} \quad && P \;=\; \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60\,000} \quad \text{[kW]} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{4. Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)} \quad && SEC \;=\; \dfrac{P}{Q} \quad \text{[kWh/kg or kWh/ton]} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{5. Throughput (screw press, continuous)} \quad && Q \;=\; 60 \cdot \rho_b \cdot A_d \cdot v_e \quad \text{[kg/hr]} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{6. Screw Flight Capacity} \quad && Q_s \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D_o^2 - D_c^2\right) \cdot s \cdot N \cdot \rho_b \cdot 60 \quad \text{[kg/hr]} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{7. Torque on Extruder Screw} \quad && T \;=\; F \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi) \cdot \dfrac{D}{2} \quad \text{[N·m]} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{8. Die Pressure Drop (Hagen–Poiseuille analogy)} \quad && \Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{32\,\mu\,L\,v}{D^2} \quad \text{[Pa]} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{9. Density Ratio (DR)} \quad && DR \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_f} \quad \text{(dimensionless)} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{10. Briquetting Yield} \quad && \eta_b \;=\; \dfrac{M_{briquette}}{M_{feed, \, dry}} \cdot 100 \quad \text{[\%]}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Symbol Table

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| L_f, L_b | Length of feed column / briquette | m |
| V_f, V_b | Volume of feed / briquette | m³ |
| ρ_f, ρ_b | Density of feed / briquette | kg/m³ |
| F | Ram force | N |
| A | Die cross-sectional area | m² |
| p_s | Specific pressure | kg/cm² |
| N | Screw speed | rpm |
| T | Torque | N·m |
| P | Power | kW |
| Q | Throughput | kg/hr |
| A_d | Die exit area | m² |
| v_e | Extrusion velocity | m/s |
| D_o, D_c | Screw outer / core diameter | m |
| s | Screw pitch | m |
| α | Screw helix angle | rad (°) |
| φ | Friction angle between feed & barrel | rad (°) |
| μ | Effective viscosity of densified feed | Pa·s |
| L, D | Die length / diameter | m |
| v | Linear velocity of material in die | m/s |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rural electrification via gasification** → Briquettes from rice husk power 50–250 kWe downdraft gasifiers in Kerala/Karnataka rice mills.
- **Industrial boiler co-firing** → 5–10% biomass briquette substitution in coal-fired CFBC boilers.
- **Domestic smokeless fuel** → Replacing wood and cow-dung cakes, reducing indoor air pollution (WHO priority).
- **Steel sector** → Coking coal briquettes via roller press with pitch binder.
- **Cement kilns** → RDF (Refuse-Derived Fuel) briquettes replacing 20% pet-coke.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The *compression ratio* and *die L/D ratio* are mathematically linked but physically distinct. CR is a *feedstock property decision*; L/D is a *machine geometry decision*. CR fixes the *amount* of densification needed; L/D determines *how* the die delivers that densification within the residence time.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Compression Ratio from First Principles

### Statement
Show that the volumetric compression ratio equals the density ratio of briquette to feed.

### Derivation

We start from the **law of conservation of mass** during densification (no material is added or removed):

$$
m_{feed} \;=\; m_{briquette}
$$

For a fixed mass basis, mass equals density times volume:

$$
\rho_f \cdot V_f \;=\; \rho_b \cdot V_b
$$

Rearranging to express the ratio of volumes:

$$
\dfrac{V_f}{V_b} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_f}
$$

By the **definition of compression ratio (CR)**—the ratio of original (feed) volume to final (briquette) volume:

$$
CR \;\equiv\; \dfrac{V_f}{V_b}
$$

Therefore, by substitution:

$$
\boxed{\,CR \;=\; \dfrac{V_f}{V_b} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_f}\,}
$$

### Engineering Interpretation
If loose sawdust has ρ_f = 150 kg/m³ and a target briquette density is ρ_b = 1100 kg/m³, then:

$$
CR \;=\; \dfrac{1100}{150} \;\approx\; 7.33
$$

This means each 1 m³ of loose sawdust must be compressed into **0.136 m³** of briquette. A CR of 7–8 is typical for screw-press biomass briquetting.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Die L/D Ratio in Terms of Compression Ratio

### Statement
Derive the relationship between die L/D ratio, compression ratio, and density ratio for a cylindrical briquette.

### Geometric Setup

Consider a single die hole of diameter *D* and length *L*. Feed enters with cross-section A_f and exits as briquette of cross-section A_b = πD²/4.

The **feed column length** (loose material that will be compressed to fill the die) is:

$$
L_f \;=\; \dfrac{CR \cdot V_b}{A_f} \;=\; \dfrac{CR \cdot \pi D^2 / 4}{A_f}
$$

Since A_f = A_b (the same die), the densified length L_b = L (the die length itself). The compression ratio expressed geometrically along the die axis:

$$
CR \;=\; \dfrac{L_f}{L} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad L_f \;=\; CR \cdot L
$$

For the material to fully densify inside the die, the feed column must compact to length L. The **effective die L/D ratio** is:

$$
\boxed{\,\dfrac{L}{D} \;=\; \dfrac{L_f}{D \cdot CR} \,}
$$

### Numerical Example
Given: CR = 6, target die diameter D = 50 mm, target briquette density 1000 kg/m³.

For proper densification, choose L/D = 5 (standard for screw press):

$$
L \;=\; 5 \times 50 \;=\; 250 \text{ mm}
$$

The feed column required to fill this die is:

$$
L_f \;=\; 6 \times 250 \;=\; 1500 \text{ mm} \;=\; 1.5 \text{ m}
$$

The screw must therefore compress 1.5 m of loose biomass into 0.25 m of briquette—achievable only with a properly tapered (converging) screw geometry.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Required to Drive a Screw Extruder

### Statement
Derive the equation for the power required at the screw shaft of a briquetting extruder.

### Derivation

A screw rotating at N rpm exerts a tangential force on the densifying material via the flight face. The mechanical power is the product of torque and angular velocity:

$$
P_{shaft} \;=\; T \cdot \omega
$$

where ω is the angular velocity in rad/s. Converting rpm to rad/s:

$$
\omega \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_{shaft} \;=\; T \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \text{[W]}
$$

Converting to kilowatts (and multiplying torque in N·m by 1/1000):

$$
\boxed{\,P \;=\; \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60\,000} \quad \text{[kW]}\,}
$$

### Derivation of Torque from Screw Geometry

The torque resisting screw rotation arises from the material's compressive reaction F and the friction at the barrel wall. On an inclined flight of helix angle α and effective radius D/2:

$$
T \;=\; F \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi) \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}
$$

where:
- F = axial compressive force on the material (N)
- α = screw helix angle (rad)
- φ = friction angle between feed and barrel surface (rad)
- D = screw outer diameter (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** As material advances toward the die, α (helix angle) decreases (screw tapers), making the screw "self-locking" near the die end. This is what generates the high back-pressure for densification—and why torque (and hence power) demand rises sharply near the die.

### Numerical Worked Example

**Given:**
- Screw speed N = 60 rpm
- Effective torque on screw T = 450 N·m
- Briquetting pressure F = 80,000 N
- Screw diameter D = 0.15 m
- Friction angle φ = 20° = 0.349 rad
- Helix angle α = 18° = 0.314 rad (typical for tapered end)

**Step 1 — Power:**

$$
P \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi \times 60 \times 450}{60\,000} \;=\; \dfrac{169646}{60\,000} \;=\; 2.827 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify with force-friction relation:**

$$
T_{calc} \;=\; 80\,000 \times \tan(0.314 + 0.349) \times \dfrac{0.15}{2}
$$

$$
T_{calc} \;=\; 80\,000 \times \tan(0.663) \times 0.075
$$

$$
T_{calc} \;=\; 80\,000 \times 0.789 \times 0.075 \;=\; 4734 \text{ N·m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The 450 N·m in Step 1 is an *operational* (steady-state) torque, while Step 2 gives a *peak* torque at the die entry. KTU students often confuse these—**show both** in design problems and apply a **service factor of 1.5–2.0** when sizing the motor:

$$
P_{motor} \;=\; 1.5 \times 2.827 \;\approx\; 4.24 \text{ kW}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Throughput of a Continuous Screw Press

### Statement
Derive the throughput (mass flow rate) equation for a continuous screw briquetting press.

### Derivation

The volume of material displaced per screw revolution equals the **screw flight volume per turn**:

$$
V_{turn} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( D_o^2 - D_c^2 \right) \cdot s \quad \text{[m³/rev]}
$$

where:
- D_o = outer (root of barrel) diameter (m)
- D_c = core (shaft) diameter (m)
- s = screw pitch (m)

The number of revolutions per hour is 60·N (with N in rpm):

$$
V_{hour} \;=\; 60 \cdot N \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( D_o^2 - D_c^2 \right) \cdot s
$$

Multiplying by the **bulk density of the briquette** (because the material in the metering zone is at briquette density):

$$
Q_{theoretical} \;=\; \dfrac{60 \pi N \rho_b s \left( D_o^2 - D_c^2 \right)}{4} \quad \text{[kg/hr]}
$$

Applying a **fill factor η_f** (typically 0.3–0.5 for tapered briquetting screws due to compression and slip-back):

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{actual} \;=\; \eta_f \cdot \dfrac{60 \pi N \rho_b s \left( D_o^2 - D_c^2 \right)}{4} \quad \text{[kg/hr]}\,}
$$

### Numerical Worked Example

**Given:**
- D_o = 0.15 m, D_c = 0.06 m
- s = 0.075 m (pitch = D_o/2 for standard extruder)
- N = 60 rpm
- ρ_b = 1100 kg/m³
- η_f = 0.4

**Computation:**

$$
Q_{act} \;=\; 0.4 \times \dfrac{60 \times \pi \times 60 \times 1100 \times 0.075 \times (0.15^2 - 0.06^2)}{4}
$$

$$
0.15^2 \;=\; 0.0225,\quad 0.06^2 \;=\; 0.0036,\quad 0.0225 - 0.0036 \;=\; 0.0189 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
Q_{act} \;=\; 0.4 \times \dfrac{60 \times 3.1416 \times 60 \times 1100 \times 0.075 \times 0.0189}{4}
$$

$$
Q_{act} \;=\; 0.4 \times \dfrac{60 \times 3.1416 \times 60 \times 1100 \times 0.075 \times 0.0189}{4}
$$

Numerator: 60 × 3.1416 × 60 = 11,309.7; × 1100 = 12,440,729; × 0.075 = 933,054.7; × 0.0189 = 17,634.7

$$
Q_{act} \;=\; 0.4 \times \dfrac{17\,634.7}{4} \;=\; 0.4 \times 4408.7 \;\approx\; 1763 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

So a 150 mm screw running at 60 rpm delivers ~1.76 ton/hr of briquettes—consistent with industrial ratings.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

### Statement
Derive the Specific Energy Consumption metric and show its typical range.

### Derivation

SEC is the energy consumed per unit mass of briquette produced:

$$
SEC \;=\; \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{Q} \quad \text{[kWh/kg]}
$$

For industrial reporting, multiply numerator and denominator by 1000 to get kWh/ton:

$$
SEC \;=\; \dfrac{1000 \cdot P}{Q} \quad \text{[kWh/ton]}
$$

### Typical SEC Values (KTU standard data)

| Machine Type | SEC (kWh/ton) |
|---|---|
| Roller press | 20–40 |
| Piston press (mechanical) | 30–60 |
| Screw press (biomass) | 40–80 |
| Hydraulic press | 50–90 |
| Pneumatic / manual | < 20 |

### Numerical Example
Continuing from §3.3 and §3.4:
- P_shaft = 2.827 kW
- Q = 1763 kg/hr

$$
SEC \;=\; \dfrac{2.827}{1763} \times 1000 \;\approx\; 1.60 \text{ kWh/kg} \;=\; 1600 \text{ kWh/ton}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** The 1.6 kWh/kg above is the *shaft SEC* (after gear reduction). A typical motor-to-shaft efficiency of 90% gives the *wall-plug* SEC = 1.6/0.9 ≈ 1.78 kWh/kg ≈ 1780 kWh/ton. The latter number is what appears in plant energy audits. KTU questions often state "motor rating"—read the problem carefully to know whether to include efficiency.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Die Pressure Drop (Hagen–Poiseuille Analogy)

### Statement
Estimate the pressure drop of densified biomass flowing through a cylindrical die of length L and diameter D, treating it as a viscous plug.

### Derivation

For a viscous material flowing as a plug in a cylindrical channel, the **Hagen–Poiseuille** equation for pressure drop per unit length is:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} \;=\; \dfrac{32\,\mu\,v}{D^2}
$$

For a die of length L:

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{32\,\mu\,L\,v}{D^2} \quad \text{[Pa]}\,}
$$

This is the **back-pressure** the screw must overcome. Higher L (longer die) and higher viscosity (denser feed, more lignin) increase ΔP dramatically—this is why very long dies stall small screws.

### Numerical Example
- μ = 10⁵ Pa·s (densified biomass at 200°C, typical)
- L = 0.25 m, D = 0.05 m
- v = 0.01 m/s (extrusion speed)

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{32 \times 10^5 \times 0.25 \times 0.01}{(0.05)^2} \;=\; \dfrac{8000}{0.0025} \;=\; 3.2 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \;=\; 3.2 \text{ MPa}
$$

This corresponds to a specific pressure of ~32 kg/cm²—reasonable for a small-die screw press.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PGAET753]**
List any **three** criteria for the selection of a briquetting machine for biomass waste, and justify each in one sentence.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Moisture content of feedstock:** A moisture content of 8–12% (wb) is optimum for a screw extruder since natural lignin acts as a binder. Above 15%, pre-drying is mandatory regardless of machine type.
2. **Lignin content of feedstock:** Feedstocks with lignin > 20% (e.g., sawdust, groundnut shell) self-bind under heat and pressure, eliminating the need for external binders in a screw press.
3. **Throughput requirement:** For < 100 kg/hr, a manual/pneumatic piston press is economical; for > 2000 kg/hr, a roller press or industrial twin-screw is the only viable architecture.
*(1 mark each for valid criterion + justification = 3 marks)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, PGAET753]**
Define **Compression Ratio (CR)** in briquetting and state its typical range for biomass briquettes.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Compression Ratio is the ratio of the volume (or length) of the loose feed material to the volume (or length) of the resulting briquette produced from the same mass. Mathematically, CR = V_f / V_b = ρ_b / ρ_f. It quantifies the degree of densification achieved during briquetting.
- **Typical range (1 mark):** For biomass briquettes (sawdust, rice husk, groundnut shell), the compression ratio typically lies in the range of **5 to 8**, corresponding to briquette densities of 900–1200 kg/m³ from feed bulk densities of 100–200 kg/m³.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PGAET753 Module 2]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the working principle, specific pressure, throughput range, and suitable feedstock for **Piston press**, **Screw press**, and **Roller press** briquetting machines. Use a comparative table.

**(b) [7 marks]** A sawdust briquetting screw press has the following specifications:
- Screw outer diameter D_o = 120 mm
- Screw core diameter D_c = 50 mm
- Screw pitch s = 60 mm
- Screw speed N = 70 rpm
- Briquette density ρ_b = 1050 kg/m³
- Fill factor η_f = 0.40

Calculate the **(i)** theoretical screw flight volume per turn, **(ii)** actual throughput in kg/hr, and **(iii)** specific energy consumption in kWh/ton, given that the operating torque on the screw is 320 N·m.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| Parameter | Piston (Ram) Press | Screw Press (Extruder) | Roller Press |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Working principle** | A reciprocating ram pushes feed through a die in a forward stroke, then retracts for the next charge | A continuously rotating tapered screw conveys and compresses feed axially through a die | Two counter-rotating rollers with shaped pockets nip the feed and compact it between them |
| **Specific pressure** | 500–1500 kg/cm² | 1000–2500 kg/cm² | 200–600 kg/cm² |
| **Throughput** | 50–300 kg/hr (intermittent) | 200–2500 kg/hr (continuous) | 500–5000 kg/hr (continuous) |
| **Suitable feedstock** | Charcoal fines, rice husk, sawdust with binder | Sawdust, groundnut shell, bagasse (lignin-rich) | Coal/coke fines, mineral ores, low-lignin biomass |
| **Energy** | 30–60 kWh/ton | 40–80 kWh/ton | 20–40 kWh/ton |

*(1.5 marks for principle + 1.5 marks for pressure + 1.5 marks for throughput + 1.5 marks for feedstock = 6 marks; 1 mark for overall table presentation and clarity)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** D_o = 0.120 m, D_c = 0.050 m, s = 0.060 m, N = 70 rpm, ρ_b = 1050 kg/m³, η_f = 0.40, T = 320 N·m.

**(i) Screw flight volume per turn [2 marks]:**

$$
V_{turn} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D_o^2 - D_c^2\right) \cdot s
$$

$$
V_{turn} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(0.120^2 - 0.050^2\right) \cdot 0.060
$$

$$
V_{turn} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(0.0144 - 0.0025\right) \cdot 0.060
$$

$$
V_{turn} \;=\; \dfrac{3.1416}{4} \times 0.0119 \times 0.060 \;=\; 0.7854 \times 0.0119 \times 0.060
$$

$$
V_{turn} \;=\; 5.607 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m³/rev}
$$

**(ii) Actual throughput [3 marks]:**

$$
Q_{act} \;=\; \eta_f \cdot 60 \cdot N \cdot V_{turn} \cdot \rho_b
$$

$$
Q_{act} \;=\; 0.40 \times 60 \times 70 \times 5.607 \times 10^{-4} \times 1050
$$

$$
Q_{act} \;=\; 0.40 \times 60 \times 70 \times 5.607 \times 10^{-4} \times 1050
$$

Step by step: 60 × 70 = 4200; × 5.607 × 10⁻⁴ = 2.355 m³/hr; × 1050 = 2472.6 kg/hr; × 0.40 = **989.0 kg/hr**

**(iii) Specific energy consumption [2 marks]:**

Power at screw shaft:

$$
P \;=\; \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60\,000} \;=\; \dfrac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 70 \times 320}{60\,000}
$$

$$
P \;=\; \dfrac{140\,633}{60\,000} \;=\; 2.344 \text{ kW}
$$

SEC:

$$
SEC \;=\; \dfrac{P}{Q} \times 1000 \;=\; \dfrac{2.344}{989.0} \times 1000
$$

$$
\boxed{\,SEC \;\approx\; 2.37 \text{ kWh/ton}\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Many students **forget the fill factor η_f** in the throughput equation—deduct 1 mark if missing.
> - Students often compute SEC in **kWh/kg** instead of **kWh/ton**—verify the unit asked. Multiplying by 1000 converts kg → ton.
> - Do NOT include motor efficiency unless the question states "motor rating." The 2.344 kW is *shaft power*; the motor would need to be 2.344/0.90 ≈ 2.6 kW.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PGAET753 Module 2]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the **functional architecture of a screw-type briquetting machine**. Label the **pre-compression zone**, **densification zone**, **die block**, and **cutter** clearly. Discuss the role of the **tapered screw flight** in generating back-pressure for briquette formation.

**(b) [7 marks]** A biomass feed has a bulk density of 180 kg/m³. The briquetting machine is required to produce cylindrical briquettes of 50 mm diameter, bulk density 1000 kg/m³, using a die with an L/D ratio of 5. Calculate:
- **(i)** the compression ratio,
- **(ii)** the die length,
- **(iii)** the length of the feed column that will be densified to one briquette of length equal to the die length,
- **(iv)** the volume of the briquette and the original volume of feed required.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram (4 marks):**

```
  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
  │   HOPPER     │───▶│  FEEDER /    │───▶│  PRE-COMPRESSION │───▶│ TAPERED      │───▶│ DIE BLOCK    │
  │              │    │  CONDITIONER │    │  ZONE            │    │ SCREW FLIGHT │    │ (L/D = 4–7)  │
  └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘
                                                                                            │
                                                                                            ▼
                                                                                   ┌──────────────────┐
                                                                                   │ HEATING SLEEVE   │
                                                                                   │ 200–280 °C       │
                                                                                   └──────────────────┘
                                                                                            │
                                                                                            ▼
                                                                                   ┌──────────────────┐
                                                                                   │ BRIQUETTE EXIT   │
                                                                                   │  + CUTTER        │
                                                                                   └──────────────────┘
```

**Functional Description (3 marks):**
- **Hopper & Conditioner:** Receives ground biomass (< 6 mm, 8–12% MC) and gently stirs to preheat.
- **Pre-compression zone:** A short, constant-pitch screw section that compacts the loose feed and expels air.
- **Tapered screw flight:** The screw diameter tapers (or flight depth reduces) toward the die, progressively decreasing the free volume. This creates **axial back-pressure** that densifies the biomass. The decreasing helix angle increases the *self-locking tendency*, which is what compacts the material against the die.
- **Die block:** The final cylindrical restriction where the densified plug is heated to 200–280 °C by **frictional + external heat**, plasticizing the lignin to act as a natural binder.
- **Cutter:** Trims the continuous extruded log into briquette lengths.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** ρ_f = 180 kg/m³, ρ_b = 1000 kg/m³, D = 50 mm = 0.05 m, L/D = 5.

**(i) Compression ratio [2 marks]:**

$$
CR \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_f} \;=\; \dfrac{1000}{180} \;=\; 5.56
$$

**(ii) Die length [1 mark]:**

$$
L \;=\; 5 \times D \;=\; 5 \times 50 \text{ mm} \;=\; 250 \text{ mm} \;=\; 0.25 \text{ m}
$$

**(iii) Feed column length for one briquette [2 marks]:**

$$
L_f \;=\; CR \cdot L \;=\; 5.56 \times 0.25 \;=\; 1.389 \text{ m}
$$

**(iv) Volumes [2 marks]:**

Briquette volume (cylinder of D = 0.05 m, L = 0.25 m):

$$
V_b \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.05)^2 \times 0.25
$$

$$
V_b \;=\; 0.7854 \times 0.0025 \times 0.25 \;=\; 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

Original feed volume (for the same mass):

$$
V_f \;=\; CR \times V_b \;=\; 5.56 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 2.730 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Why Students Lose Marks Here:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert D from mm to m** before substituting into volume formula — entire numerical answer becomes wrong by 10⁶. (Common 1-mark loss.)
> 2. **Mixing up CR definition**: Some textbooks define CR as V_b/V_f (ratio of compressed to original). KTU standard convention is **CR = V_f/V_b = ρ_b/ρ_f**. Always state the convention at the start of your answer.
> 3. **Omitting the L_f result** as a sanity check: physically, the feed column (1.39 m) being compressed to a briquette of 0.25 m is plausible for biomass — if you got L_f < L, your CR direction is wrong.
> 4. **Not showing intermediate arithmetic**: KTU examiners award step-marks. Even if the final answer is wrong, showing the formula substitution earns 50% of the marks for that sub-part.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before exam):**

- ✅ Briquetting = mechanical densification of loose waste to a compact, high-density solid fuel.
- ✅ Three main machine types: **Piston (ram)**, **Screw (extruder)**, **Roller press**.
- ✅ Selection depends on: **MC%**, **lignin content**, **throughput**, **product shape**, **abrasive index**.
- ✅ MC = 8–12% (wb) is the universal optimum; < 8% needs binder, > 15% needs drying.
- ✅ Lignin > 20% → self-binding (screw extruder) — no external binder needed.
- ✅ **Compression Ratio:** CR = V_f / V_b = ρ_b / ρ_f (dimensionless, typically 5–8 for biomass).
- ✅ **Die L/D ratio:** typically 4–7 for screw extruders.
- ✅ **Power equation:** P = 2πNT / 60 000 (kW, with T in N·m, N in rpm).
- ✅ **Throughput equation:** Q_act = η_f · 60 · N · V_turn · ρ_b (kg/hr).
- ✅ **SEC range:** 20–90 kWh/ton (roller < piston < screw < hydraulic).
- ✅ **Motor sizing:** Always apply a **service factor of 1.5–2.0** above calculated shaft power.
- ✅ Hagen–Poiseuille analogy gives die pressure drop: ΔP = 32 μ L v / D².
- ✅ Screw helix angle α decreases (taper increases) toward die → self-locking → higher back-pressure.
- ✅ Self-heating in screw press: 200–280 °C due to friction, plasticizing lignin into natural binder.
- ✅ Particle size < 6 mm is essential to avoid die blockage and excessive wear.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark questions: **always pair Selection (qualitative table) with Design (quantitative calculation)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Utilization of shell, stem and stalk: Production of activated carbon
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Utilization of shell, stem and stalk: Production of activated carbon

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Activated Carbon (AC)** is a highly porous, amorphous carbonaceous adsorbent material characterized by an exceptionally high specific surface area (typically **500–1500 m²/g**), well-developed internal pore structure, and a high degree of surface reactivity. It is produced by the thermal decomposition and controlled activation of carbon-rich precursor materials of plant or animal origin.

In the context of KTU Module 2 (Waste and Byproduct Utilization), **agricultural residues** — specifically **shells** (e.g., coconut shell, walnut shell, almond shell, groundnut shell), **stems** (e.g., cotton stem, jute stem), and **stalks** (e.g., corn stalk, rice straw, wheat straw, sugarcane bagasse, sorghum stalk) — are recognized as **premium feedstocks** for activated carbon manufacture because they are lignocellulosic, abundantly available, renewable, low-cost, and rich in fixed carbon content (typically **15–25%** on a dry basis).

> [!NOTE]
> **Lignocellulosic Biomass** — A complex biopolymer composite composed of three main structural components: **cellulose** (35–50%), **hemicellulose** (20–35%), and **lignin** (15–30%). The relative proportion of these components determines the yield, porosity, and mechanical strength of the resulting activated carbon.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge of Carbon"

Imagine a **sponge made entirely of carbon atoms**. When you soak a normal kitchen sponge in water, it absorbs a little. But this "carbon sponge" has millions of microscopic tunnels and cavities inside it, so when polluted water passes through, the contaminants get **stuck (adsorbed)** to the inner walls.

**The "activation" process is what creates those tunnels.** Raw agricultural waste is dense and has very few pores. When we heat it carefully and "activate" it with steam or chemicals, we essentially **carve out a network of nano-sized pores** — like drilling millions of tiny wells into a solid block. The result is a material that is mostly empty space internally, giving it a massive internal surface area (imagine unfolding a football field inside a teaspoon!).

> [!TIP]
> A single gram of high-quality activated carbon can have a surface area equivalent to **1,000–3,000 m²** — roughly the area of half a football pitch, all packed into something that weighs less than a paperclip.

## 1.3 Why Agricultural Wastes Are Ideal Feedstocks

| Feedstock Category | Example | Fixed Carbon (% dry) | Key Advantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shells** | Coconut shell | **22–25%** | Highest density, hardest shell, yields premium microporous AC |
| **Shells** | Groundnut/Peanut shell | 18–22% | High lignin, good abrasion resistance |
| **Shells** | Walnut shell | 20–24% | Naturally hard structure, good mesopore development |
| **Stalks** | Rice straw | 15–18% | Highest global annual production, cheap |
| **Stalks** | Wheat straw | 17–20% | Good pore development after chemical activation |
| **Stalks** | Sugarcane bagasse | 18–22% | Byproduct of sugar industry, abundant |
| **Stalks** | Corn stalk | 17–21% | Lignin-rich, good for high-yield AC |
| **Stems** | Cotton stalk | 19–23% | Fibrous, gives high mechanical strength AC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **carbon content and density** of the precursor directly correlate with the **yield** and **mechanical strength** of the final activated carbon. Coconut shell, being exceptionally dense and lignin-rich, produces the most premium-grade granular activated carbon (GAC) used in gold recovery and potable water filters worldwide.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Stage Manufacturing Logic

Production of activated carbon from lignocellulosic wastes follows a **sequential two-stage logic**: (1) **Carbonization (Pyrolysis)** followed by (2) **Activation**. Each stage has a distinct chemical and physical purpose.

### Stage 1 — Carbonization (Pyrolysis)
- **Purpose:** Convert the raw biomass into a carbon-rich **char** by removing volatile matter (water, CO, CO₂, CH₄, tars, acetic acid).
- **Conditions:** Temperature **400–800 °C**, in an **inert atmosphere** (N₂ or Ar), heating rate **5–20 °C/min**, residence time **1–3 hours**.
- **Chemistry (simplified):**

$$\text{Biomass (C, H, O, N)} \xrightarrow{\Delta, \, \text{inert}} \text{Char (C)} + \text{Volatiles} + \text{Tar}$$

- **Outcome:** A primary carbon skeleton (fixed carbon **~60–80%** of char mass) with limited, mostly closed/non-accessible porosity.

### Stage 2 — Activation
This stage opens up the closed pores and creates new ones. There are two industrial routes:

#### (a) Physical Activation (Thermal)
- **Agent:** Steam (H₂O) or Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)
- **Temperature:** **800–1000 °C**
- **Time:** 1–4 hours
- **Reactions (gasification):**

$$\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +131.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} \quad \Delta H = +172.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

- **Mechanism:** Selective gasification of disordered/less-stable carbon atoms widens existing pores and creates new micro/mesopores. Endothermic reaction — externally controlled.

#### (b) Chemical Activation
- **Agents:** ZnCl₂, H₃PO₄, KOH, K₂CO₃, NaOH
- **Impregnation:** Precursor soaked in concentrated chemical solution (Impregnation Ratio **IR = 0.5–3.0**).
- **Temperature:** **400–800 °C** (lower than physical activation).
- **Mechanism:** Chemical acts as a **dehydrating / oxidizing / pore-forming template**; prevents tar formation; promotes aromatic condensation.
- **Key reaction with KOH (modern high-surface-area route):**

$$6\text{KOH} + 2\text{C} \rightarrow 2\text{K} + 3\text{H}_2 + 2\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3$$

- **Advantage:** Higher yield, lower temperature, more uniform pore size.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Process Yield** | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{AC}}{m_{precursor}} \times 100$ | % | Mass of final AC over mass of dry precursor |
| 2 | **Burn-off** | $\text{BO} = \dfrac{m_{char} - m_{AC}}{m_{char}} \times 100$ | % | Mass loss during activation step |
| 3 | **Impregnation Ratio (IR)** | $\text{IR} = \dfrac{m_{chem}}{m_{precursor}}$ | g/g (or mass ratio) | Critical for chemical activation |
| 4 | **Carbonization Efficiency** | $\eta_C = \dfrac{m_{char}}{m_{precursor}} \times 100$ | % | Independent of activation |
| 5 | **BET Surface Area (linear form)** | $\dfrac{P/P_0}{V(1 - P/P_0)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C-1}{V_m C}\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$ | — | Plot left side vs. $P/P_0$; slope + intercept give $V_m$ |
| 6 | **BET Surface Area** | $S_{BET} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot N_A \cdot a_m}{V_{molar}}$ | m²/g | $a_m = 0.162 \text{ nm}^2$ for N₂ |
| 7 | **Langmuir Isotherm** | $q_e = \dfrac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$ | mg/g | Monolayer adsorption capacity $q_m$ |
| 8 | **Freundlich Isotherm** | $q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}$ | mg/g | Heterogeneous surface; $1/n$ between 0 and 1 |
| 9 | **Iodine Number (IN)** | $\text{IN} = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) \cdot V \cdot 126.91}{m_{AC}}$ | mg/g | Indicator of **micropore** content (< 2 nm) |
| 10 | **Methylene Blue Number (MBN)** | $\text{MBN} = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) \cdot V \cdot 319.86}{m_{AC}}$ | mg/g | Indicator of **mesopore** content (2–50 nm) |
| 11 | **Ash Content** | $\text{Ash\%} = \dfrac{m_{ash}}{m_{AC}} \times 100$ | % | Quality indicator; < 5% preferred |
| 12 | **Activation Energy (Arrhenius)** | $k = A \, e^{-E_a/RT}$ | kJ/mol | $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ |
| 13 | **Total Pore Volume** | $V_t = \dfrac{1}{\rho_{true}} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_{bulk}}$ | cm³/g | From He and Hg pycnometry |
| 14 | **Average Pore Width (DFT)** | $L_0 = \dfrac{4 V_t}{S_{BET}}$ | nm | Classifies micro/meso/macropore |

> [!NOTE]
> In the BET equation, $V$ = volume of N₂ adsorbed at relative pressure $P/P_0$; $V_m$ = monolayer volume; $C$ = BET constant related to adsorbate-adsorbent interaction energy. The iodine number specifically probes pores of diameter **> 0.5 nm**, while the methylene blue number probes pores **> 1.5 nm**.

## 2.3 Why "Why" and "How" — Engineering Significance

- **Why two stages?** A single heating step cannot simultaneously devolatilize the biomass AND develop fine pore structure. Carbonization first builds the rigid carbon skeleton, then activation selectively etches it.
- **Why chemical activation gives higher yield?** The chemical (e.g., H₃PO₄) **inhibits tar formation**, retains more carbon in the solid, and acts as a **template** around which pores form. When washed out, those template sites become pores.
- **Why coconut shell is the gold standard?** Its naturally high **lignin** content (≈ 35%) gives a rigid, abrasion-resistant char; its high **density** means more carbon per unit volume, and its natural microporous structure aligns perfectly with the activation process.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Engineering Use:** Coconut shell-based activated carbon is the industry standard for **gold ore processing (CIP/CIL circuits)**, **drinking water purification**, **medical-grade hemodialysis filters**, and **air purification in nuclear submarines**. The same process applied to rice straw yields a cheaper AC primarily used in **industrial wastewater treatment** (dyes, phenols, heavy metals).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Process Yield of Activated Carbon (Coconut Shell, Two-Stage Route)

**Given Problem (typical KTU-style numerical):**
500 g of dried coconut shell (moisture = 5% w.b., ash = 1.5% d.b.) is carbonized in a muffle furnace at 600 °C under N₂ flow, producing 180 g of char. The char is then physically activated with steam at 850 °C for 2 hours, producing 108 g of activated carbon. Calculate:
(a) Carbonization efficiency
(b) Overall process yield
(c) Burn-off during activation
(d) Final ash content if the AC contains 4.5 g ash.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Determine dry mass of precursor (eliminate moisture):**

$$m_{dry} = m_{wet} \times (1 - M) = 500 \times (1 - 0.05) = 475 \text{ g}$$

where $M$ is moisture content on wet basis (0.05).

**Step 2 — Calculate Carbonization Efficiency (η_C):**

$$\eta_C = \frac{m_{char}}{m_{dry}} \times 100 = \frac{180}{475} \times 100$$

$$\eta_C = 37.89\%$$

> **Conversion logic:** Carbonization efficiency measures how much of the dry biomass is retained as solid char after pyrolysis. Volatile matter and tars are driven off. For coconut shell, a typical literature range is 30–40%.

**Step 3 — Calculate Overall Process Yield (η):**

$$\eta = \frac{m_{AC}}{m_{dry}} \times 100 = \frac{108}{475} \times 100$$

$$\eta = 22.74\%$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is the **commercial yield** — the mass fraction of original precursor that becomes the saleable product. A 22–25% yield is typical for coconut shell-based GAC via physical activation.

**Step 4 — Calculate Burn-off (BO):**

$$\text{BO} = \frac{m_{char} - m_{AC}}{m_{char}} \times 100 = \frac{180 - 108}{180} \times 100$$

$$\text{BO} = 40.00\%$$

> **Conversion logic:** Burn-off represents the fraction of char mass consumed by the activating gas (steam/CO₂). Higher burn-off generally = higher surface area, but excessive burn-off (>50%) degrades mechanical strength.

**Step 5 — Calculate Final Ash Content (Ash%):**

$$\text{Ash\%} = \frac{m_{ash}}{m_{AC}} \times 100 = \frac{4.5}{108} \times 100$$

$$\text{Ash\%} = 4.17\%$$

> **Conversion logic:** Ash (inorganic residue, mostly silica, alumina, K, Ca) reduces adsorption capacity. Premium-grade AC has **Ash% < 5%**, which our result satisfies.

### Final Result Table

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbonization Efficiency | $\eta_C$ | **37.89 %** | Normal (literature: 30–40%) |
| Overall Process Yield | $\eta$ | **22.74 %** | Normal (literature: 20–28%) |
| Activation Burn-off | BO | **40.00 %** | Optimal (literature: 30–50%) |
| Final Ash Content | Ash% | **4.17 %** | Premium (< 5%) |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Iodine Number Calculation (Quality Assessment)

**Problem:** A 0.500 g sample of activated carbon produced from rice straw is added to 50.0 mL of a 0.0500 N iodine solution. After equilibration, the filtrate is titrated against 0.100 N sodium thiosulfate and requires 18.20 mL. A blank (no AC) requires 25.00 mL of the same thiosulfate. Calculate the **Iodine Number (mg I₂ adsorbed per g of AC)**.

**Step 1 — Moles of thiosulfate used in blank:**

$$n_{blank} = N \times V = 0.100 \times 25.00 = 2.500 \text{ meq} = 2.500 \text{ mmol}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of thiosulfate used in sample titration:**

$$n_{sample} = 0.100 \times 18.20 = 1.820 \text{ mmol}$$

**Step 3 — Moles of I₂ remaining in solution (unadsorbed):**

The reaction: $\text{I}_2 + 2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3^{2-} \rightarrow 2\text{I}^- + \text{S}_4\text{O}_6^{2-}$

So 1 mol I₂ ≡ 2 mol thiosulfate.

$$n_{I_2, unadsorbed} = \frac{n_{sample}}{2} = \frac{1.820}{2} = 0.910 \text{ mmol}$$

**Step 4 — Moles of I₂ initially added:**

$$n_{I_2, initial} = \frac{n_{blank}}{2} = \frac{2.500}{2} = 1.250 \text{ mmol}$$

**Step 5 — Moles of I₂ adsorbed onto AC:**

$$n_{adsorbed} = 1.250 - 0.910 = 0.340 \text{ mmol}$$

**Step 6 — Mass of I₂ adsorbed (M = 253.81 g/mol):**

$$m_{adsorbed} = 0.340 \times 10^{-3} \times 253.81 = 0.08629 \text{ g} = 86.29 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 7 — Iodine Number:**

$$\text{IN} = \frac{m_{adsorbed}}{m_{AC}} = \frac{86.29}{0.500}$$

$$\boxed{\text{IN} = 172.6 \text{ mg/g}}$$

> **Interpretation:** A value of ~170 mg/g is classified as **low-grade**. Premium coconut-shell AC typically gives **1000–1200 mg/g**. This indicates the rice-straw AC prepared has limited micropore development and would not be suitable for potable water treatment.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — BET Surface Area from Gas Adsorption Data

**Problem:** At 77 K, the following N₂ adsorption data are recorded on a rice-stalk AC (sample mass = 0.200 g):

| $P/P_0$ | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.30 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{ads}$ (cm³ STP/g) | 180 | 220 | 270 | 310 |

Compute the BET surface area. Given: $V_m$ from linear fit = 210 cm³ STP/g; cross-sectional area of N₂ $a_m = 0.162 \text{ nm}^2 = 16.2 \times 10^{-20} \text{ m}^2$; molar volume STP = 22,414 cm³/mol.

**Step 1 — Compute monolayer volume in cm³ STP/g:**

$$V_m = 210 \text{ cm}^3 \text{ STP/g}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to moles per gram:**

$$n_m = \frac{V_m}{V_{STP}} = \frac{210}{22414} = 9.369 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/g}$$

**Step 3 — Compute number of molecules per gram:**

$$N_{molecules} = n_m \times N_A = 9.369 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}$$

$$N_{molecules} = 5.642 \times 10^{21} \text{ molecules/g}$$

**Step 4 — Compute total surface area per gram:**

$$S_{BET} = N_{molecules} \times a_m = 5.642 \times 10^{21} \times 16.2 \times 10^{-20}$$

$$S_{BET} = 5.642 \times 16.2 \times 10^{1} = 91.4 \times 10 = 914 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$$

$$\boxed{S_{BET} \approx 914 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}}$$

> **Interpretation:** A BET surface area of **~900 m²/g** is **medium-grade**. With KOH activation, rice-stalk ACs have been reported in literature up to **3000 m²/g**. This indicates further process optimization (higher KOH/precursor ratio, higher temperature) is needed.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Conditions vs. Final Properties

| Activation Method | Chemical Agent | Typical Temp (°C) | BET (m²/g) | Yield (%) | Pore Character |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Steam (physical)** | H₂O | 800–900 | 500–900 | 20–30 | Microporous |
| **CO₂ (physical)** | CO₂ | 850–1000 | 400–800 | 18–25 | Microporous |
| **ZnCl₂ (chemical)** | ZnCl₂ | 500–700 | 800–1500 | 30–45 | Micro + meso |
| **H₃PO₄ (chemical)** | H₃PO₄ | 400–600 | 900–1600 | 35–50 | Mesoporous |
| **KOH (chemical)** | KOH | 700–900 | 1500–3000 | 15–30 | Microporous (ultra-high) |
| **K₂CO₃ (chemical)** | K₂CO₃ | 700–850 | 1000–2000 | 25–35 | Microporous |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Activated Carbon Production Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AGRI WASTE INTAKE] --> B[SHREDDING AND GRINDING]
    B --> C[DRYING 100C 24H]
    C --> D[SCREENING 40 MESH]
    D --> E[STAGE 1 CARBONIZATION]
    E1[FURNACE 400-800C N2 ATMOSPHERE 1-3 HR] --> E
    E --> F[STAGE 2 ACTIVATION]
    F --> F1[PHYSICAL STEAM 800-1000C 1-4 HR]
    F --> F2[CHEMICAL H3PO4 OR KOH IMPREGNATION 400-900C]
    F1 --> G[COOLING UNDER INERT GAS]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[POST TREATMENT ACID WASH WATER WASH]
    H --> I[DRYING 110C 12H]
    I --> J[GRADING SIEVING 8x30 12x40 MESH]
    J --> K[QUALITY TEST BET IN MBN ASH]
    K --> L[PACKAGING 25 KG BAGS OR 500 KG BULK]
    L --> M[END USER WATER TREATMENT GOLD RECOVERY AIR PURIFICATION]
    style A fill:#6b8e23,color:#fff
    style M fill:#2e8b57,color:#fff
    style E fill:#cd853f,color:#fff
    style F fill:#cd853f,color:#fff
    style K fill:#4682b4,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Two-Stage Process Flow (Detailed Engineering View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[COCONUT SHELL] --> A2[DRIED CHIPS]
    A2 --> A3[PYROLYSIS 600C N2]
    A3 --> A4[CHAR 60 to 80% FIXED C]
    A4 --> A5[ACTIVATION 850C STEAM 2 HR]
    A5 --> A6[ACTIVATED CARBON]
    A6 --> A7[GRANULAR GAC 8x30 MESH]
    A6 --> A8[POWDERED PAC 200 MESH]
    A7 --> A9[AQUEOUS ADSORPTION COLUMN]
    A7 --> A10[GASEOUS ADSORPTION FILTER]
    style A1 fill:#deb887,color:#000
    style A4 fill:#8b4513,color:#fff
    style A6 fill:#000000,color:#fff
    style A9 fill:#1e90ff,color:#fff
    style A10 fill:#87ceeb,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Pore-Development Mechanism (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[RAW BIOMASS DENSE STRUCTURE NO PORES] --> P2[CARBONIZATION REMOVES VOLATILES CREATES SKELETON]
    P2 --> P3[CHAR HAS CLOSED INACCESSIBLE PORES]
    P3 --> P4[ACTIVATION SELECTIVE ETCHING BY STEAM OR CHEMICAL]
    P4 --> P5[OPEN MICROPORES 50 PERCENT]
    P4 --> P6[OPEN MESOPORES 30 PERCENT]
    P4 --> P7[MACROPORE CHANNELS 20 PERCENT]
    P5 --> P8[FINAL AC S BET 500 TO 1500 M2 PER G]
    P6 --> P8
    P7 --> P8
    style P1 fill:#8b4513,color:#fff
    style P3 fill:#654321,color:#fff
    style P8 fill:#000000,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Process Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    Q1 -->|NO| Q3{NEED HIGH YIELD 40 PERCENT}

    Q3 -->|NO| Q5{ECOFRIENDLY CHEMICAL FREE}

    Q5 -->|NO| Q7[CHOOSE ZNCL2 ACTIVATION 600C]
```
