<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> Define activated carbon. List any four agricultural feedstocks used for its production.

**Model Answer:**
Activated carbon is a highly porous carbonaceous adsorbent with a high internal surface area (500–1500 m²/g) produced by carbonization and controlled activation of carbon-rich precursors. Four agricultural feedstocks are:
1. **Coconut shell**
2. **Rice straw / Rice husk**
3. **Sugarcane bagasse**
4. **Corn stalk / Groundnut shell**

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> Differentiate between physical and chemical activation of carbon.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Physical Activation | Chemical Activation |
|---|---|---|
| Activating agent | Steam / CO₂ | ZnCl₂, H₃PO₄, KOH |
| Temperature | 800–1000 °C | 400–800 °C |
| Steps | Two (carbonization + activation) | One or two (often combined) |
| Yield | Lower (20–30%) | Higher (30–50%) |
| Surface area | Moderate (500–900 m²/g) | High (1000–3000 m²/g) |
| Pollution | Low | High (chemical recovery needed) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain the two-stage process of activated carbon manufacture from coconut shell, with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the effect of temperature on yield and surface area. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Raw Material Preparation (1 mark):**
Dried coconut shells are crushed, ground, and sieved to a uniform particle size of 1–3 mm. Moisture removed by oven drying at 110 °C for 12 h.

**Step 2 — Stage 1: Carbonization (2 marks):**
- Conducted in a stainless-steel horizontal tubular furnace.
- Temperature: **600 °C**, heating rate: **10 °C/min**, atmosphere: **N₂** (flow 100 mL/min), time: **2 h**.
- Outcome: Volatiles (CO, CO₂, CH₄, acetic acid, tars) are released; the residue is a **char** with fixed carbon ~70% and incipient closed porosity.

**Step 3 — Stage 2: Physical Activation (2 marks):**
- Char is heated to **850 °C** in presence of **steam** (H₂O vapor) at flow rate 2 g/min for 2 hours.
- Steam reacts with disordered carbon: C + H₂O → CO + H₂ (endothermic).
- Pores are opened, micropores enlarge, surface area rises from ~200 m²/g to ~900 m²/g.

**Step 4 — Effect of Temperature (2 marks):**
- **Low temperature (< 700 °C):** Incomplete carbonization, low porosity, low surface area, but high yield.
- **Optimal temperature (800–900 °C):** Maximum surface area, optimum burn-off, good yield.
- **High temperature (> 1000 °C):** Excessive burn-off, pore widening, collapse of micropores, drop in surface area, lower yield, sintering of ash.

> **(b)** A 1.00 kg sample of dried coconut shell (moisture 4%, ash 2% db) yields 380 g of char upon carbonization at 650 °C. This char is activated with steam to give 250 g of AC. The ash in the final AC is 6.25 g. Calculate: (i) Carbonization efficiency, (ii) Overall yield, (iii) Burn-off, (iv) Final ash percentage. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Dry mass of precursor:**
$$m_{dry} = 1000 \times (1 - 0.04) = 960 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Carbonization efficiency (η_C):**
$$\eta_C = \frac{380}{960} \times 100 = 39.58\%$$

**Step 3 — Overall yield (η):**
$$\eta = \frac{250}{960} \times 100 = 26.04\%$$

**Step 4 — Burn-off (BO):**
$$\text{BO} = \frac{380 - 250}{380} \times 100 = 34.21\%$$

**Step 5 — Ash percentage:**
$$\text{Ash\%} = \frac{6.25}{250} \times 100 = 2.50\%$$

**Final Answer:** $\eta_C = 39.58\%$, $\eta = 26.04\%$, BO = 34.21%, Ash% = 2.50% (premium grade).

---

> **Q3(b) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **(a)** Discuss the production of activated carbon from rice straw using **H₃PO₄ chemical activation**. Explain the role of impregnation ratio and activation temperature on pore structure. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Feedstock Preparation (1 mark):**
Rice straw is washed, sun-dried, ground, and sieved (40 mesh). Moisture removed at 105 °C for 24 h.

**Step 2 — Chemical Impregnation (2 marks):**
- Rice straw is soaked in **85% H₃PO₄** solution at room temperature for 12 h.
- **Impregnation Ratio (IR) = mass of H₃PO₄ / mass of straw = 1.0–2.0**.
- Phosphoric acid acts as a **dehydrating agent**, breaking glycosidic linkages in cellulose/hemicellulose, suppressing tar formation, and swelling the biomass.

**Step 3 — Carbonization-Activation (Single Step) (2 marks):**
- Impregnated mass heated in a furnace to **450–550 °C** at 5 °C/min under limited air/N₂ for 1–2 h.
- H₃PO₄ catalyzes aromatization and acts as a **pore template**.

**Step 4 — Washing (1 mark):**
- AC is washed repeatedly with hot distilled water until pH neutral to remove residual H₃PO₄.
- Acid can be recovered and recycled (70–80% recovery).

**Step 5 — Effect of IR and Temperature on Pore Structure (1 mark):**
- **Low IR (0.5):** Few pores, low surface area (~500 m²/g).
- **High IR (2.0):** Excessive H₃PO₄ creates more mesopores, BET rises to ~1500 m²/g, but yield falls.
- **Low temp (< 400 °C):** Incomplete activation, low surface area.
- **High temp (> 600 °C):** Pore collapse, BET drops.

> **(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **iodine number test** for activated carbon. Why is it considered an indicator of micropore content? A 0.250 g AC sample is added to 25.0 mL of 0.0400 N iodine solution. After equilibration, titration of filtrate required 14.50 mL of 0.100 N sodium thiosulfate, while the blank required 20.00 mL. Calculate the iodine number. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Iodine Number Test Procedure (2 marks):**
1. A known mass of dry AC (0.1–1.0 g) is weighed and transferred to an Erlenmeyer flask.
2. 25–50 mL of standardized iodine solution (0.05–0.10 N) is added; flask is shaken for 30 s and filtered.
3. A 10 mL aliquot of the filtrate is titrated against standardized Na₂S₂O₃ (0.10 N) using starch indicator.
4. **Blank titration** (no AC) is performed under identical conditions.

**Why it indicates micropores:** Iodine (molecular diameter ≈ 0.56 nm) can access only pores > 0.5 nm. Hence, the amount of I₂ adsorbed is a direct measure of **micropore volume** (pores < 2 nm). High IN = abundant micropores.

**Numerical Solution:**

**Step 2 — Thiosulfate used:**
- Blank: $0.100 \times 20.00 = 2.000$ meq
- Sample: $0.100 \times 14.50 = 1.450$ meq

**Step 3 — Difference (meq of I₂ adsorbed, × ½):**
$$\Delta n = \frac{2.000 - 1.450}{2} = 0.275 \text{ mmol I}_2$$

**Step 4 — Mass of I₂ adsorbed (M = 253.81):**
$$m_{I_2} = 0.275 \times 10^{-3} \times 253.81 = 0.0698 \text{ g} = 69.8 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 5 — Iodine Number:**
$$\text{IN} = \frac{69.8}{0.250} = 279.2 \text{ mg/g}$$

> **Interpretation:** ~280 mg/g is classified as low-medium grade, suitable only for industrial decolorization, not for potable water treatment.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (avoid these in the exam):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert wet mass to dry mass** when moisture is given. Always subtract moisture first.
> 2. **Confusing units of normality and molarity** in iodine number calculation. Both are valid but the equivalent factor matters.
> 3. **Not specifying N₂ atmosphere** in carbonization — examiners specifically look for "inert atmosphere" wording.
> 4. **Writing "carbon" instead of "carbonization efficiency"** — use the full technical term.
> 5. **Omitting the chemical recovery step** when discussing H₃PO₄/ZnCl₂ activation — examiner allocates 1 mark for this.
> 6. **Mixing up "adsorption" and "absorption"** — adsorption is a **surface** phenomenon, never bulk.
> 7. **Not mentioning ash content significance** — it's a quality indicator and you may be asked its typical value (< 5%).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Key Definitions:**
> - **Activated Carbon (AC):** Porous carbonaceous adsorbent, $S_{BET}$ = 500–3000 m²/g.
> - **Carbonization:** Pyrolysis at 400–800 °C in inert atmosphere to form char.
> - **Physical Activation:** Steam or CO₂ at 800–1000 °C.
> - **Chemical Activation:** ZnCl₂, H₃PO₄, KOH at 400–800 °C.

> **Premium Feedstocks (in order of commercial importance):**
> 1. **Coconut shell** → microporous GAC for potable water & gold recovery.
> 2. **Coal** (out of syllabus scope) → traditional feedstock.
> 3. **Wood** → powdered AC for decolorization.
> 4. **Rice husk / straw** → cheap AC for industrial wastewater.
> 5. **Bagasse** → sugar industry byproduct AC.

> **Key Numerical Values to Memorize:**
> - BET surface area of premium AC: **1000–1500 m²/g**
> - Iodine Number of premium AC: **≥ 1000 mg/g**
> - Methylene Blue Number: **≥ 200 mg/g**
> - Ash content: **< 5%**
> - Typical burn-off: **30–50%**
> - Typical overall yield (coconut shell): **20–28%**
> - Activation temperature: **800–900 °C** (physical); **450–700 °C** (chemical)

> **Key Reactions to Remember:**
> - C + H₂O → CO + H₂ (steam activation)
> - C + CO₂ → 2CO (CO₂ activation)
> - 6KOH + 2C → 2K + 3H₂ + 2K₂CO₃ (KOH activation)

> **Quality Indicators Hierarchy (highest to lowest micropore indicator):**
> **Iodine Number** > **BET Surface Area** > **Methylene Blue Number** > **Ash Content**

> **Advantages of Agricultural Wastes as Feedstocks:**
> - Renewable and abundant (millions of tonnes/year generated).
> - Low or negative cost — solves waste disposal problem.
> - High volatile matter → high reactivity for activation.
> - Lignocellulosic structure naturally microporous.
> - Carbon-neutral — closes the carbon cycle.

> **Limitations to Mention in Answers:**
> - High ash content in some wastes (rice husk: 15–20% ash).
> - Seasonal and geographic variability of supply.
> - Inconsistent product quality due to feedstock variability.
> - Need for pre-treatment (washing, size reduction).
> - Chemical activation requires acid recovery to prevent pollution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: By-products of agro-industries: Rice mill, oil mill, cattle feed mill
Utilization of rice husk: Production of silica and cement from rice husk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Utilization of rice husk: Production of silica and cement from rice husk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rice Husk (RH)** is an agricultural lignocellulosic byproduct generated during the milling of paddy (rice), constituting approximately **20–25%** of the total paddy weight by mass. Chemically, rice husk is composed of cellulose (35%), hemicellulose (25%), lignin (20%), and **silica (SiO₂)** in the range of **15–28%** on a dry weight basis, making it one of the most abundant silica-rich biomass residues in the world.

**Rice Husk Ash (RHA)** is the inorganic residue obtained after the complete combustion of rice husk under controlled thermal conditions. RHA is primarily composed of **amorphous silica (80–97%)** along with minor quantities of metallic oxides (K₂O, CaO, MgO, Al₂O₃, Fe₂O₃) and unburnt carbon (Loss on Ignition, LOI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Silica from Rice Husk** refers to the high-purity silicon dioxide (SiO₂) extracted from RHA through chemical (acid/alkali leaching) or thermal routes, with applications spanning construction, electronics, pharmaceuticals, and catalysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Production of silica and cement from rice husk is identified as a high-weightage topic under "Industrial Byproduct Utilization" and is frequently tested as a 14-mark Part B question in End Semester Examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of rice husk as a **"natural silica mine"** that grows in paddy fields. When rice is harvested and milled, the protective outer shell (husk) is discarded as waste. However, this "waste" is essentially a biogenic silica container.

**Real-world analogy:** Imagine boiling seawater to extract salt — the salt was always there, you just removed the water. Similarly, burning rice husk in a controlled furnace "removes" the organic matter (cellulose, lignin) and leaves behind concentrated silica. This is analogous to roasting coffee beans to obtain a more concentrated, valuable product.

The **key insight** is that the silica in rice husk exists in a hydrated, amorphous, and highly reactive form (unlike quartz sand), which makes it exceptionally useful for producing **high-grade cement** and **nano-silica**.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Recall Tip:** RH (organic + silica) → Combustion → RHA (mostly silica) → Acid wash → Pure SiO₂ → Cement or Nano-silica

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Bulk density of rice husk:** 90–150 kg/m³
- **Calorific value of rice husk:** 12–15 MJ/kg
- **Silica content in RHA:** 80–97% (amorphous form)
- **Specific surface area of nano-silica:** 50–500 m²/g
- **World rice production:** ~500 million tonnes/year → ~100 million tonnes of rice husk generated annually
- **Standard for RHA in cement (ASTM C618):** LOI < 10%, SiO₂ + Al₂O₃ + Fe₂O₃ > 70%
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Process Logic

### A. Production of Silica from Rice Husk

**Step 1: Collection and Pre-treatment**
- Rice husk is collected from rice mills and washed with deionized water to remove dust, mud, and soluble impurities.
- Dried in an oven at **100–110°C for 12–24 hours** to reduce moisture content below **10%**.

**Step 2: Combustion / Pyrolysis**
- Combustion is performed under **controlled conditions** to obtain amorphous silica.
- **Temperature range:** 500–700°C (for amorphous silica) | Above 900°C (for crystalline silica — undesirable).
- **Why this matters:** Crystalline silica (cristobalite/tridymite) is **toxic** and **less reactive**, hence combustion control is critical.

**Step 3: Acid Leaching**
- RHA is treated with **HCl (1–3 M)** at 70–90°C for 1–2 hours.
- This dissolves metallic impurities (K, Na, Ca, Mg, Fe) as soluble chlorides.
- The residue is washed with distilled water until pH = 7.
- **Chemical logic:** $M^{n+} + nHCl \rightarrow MCl_n + nH^+$

**Step 4: Alkali Extraction (Sodium Silicate Route)**
- Acid-leached ash is reacted with **NaOH (1–2 M)** at 80–100°C.
- Reaction: $SiO_2 + 2NaOH \rightarrow Na_2SiO_3 + H_2O$
- Sodium silicate solution is filtered to remove unreacted carbon.

**Step 5: Precipitation of Silica**
- Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) is added to the sodium silicate solution until pH = 4–5.
- Reaction: $Na_2SiO_3 + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow SiO_2 \downarrow + Na_2SO_4 + H_2O$
- The precipitated silica gel is aged, washed, and dried at **100–120°C**.
- Final calcination at **600–800°C** yields high-purity white silica powder.

### B. Production of Cement from Rice Husk

**Step 1: Production of RHA**
- Same as above, but combustion temperature is maintained at **600–700°C** to preserve **amorphous silica** (highly reactive pozzolan).

**Step 2: Pozzolanic Reaction**
- Amorphous SiO₂ reacts with calcium hydroxide [Ca(OH)₂] released during cement hydration:
$$SiO_2 + Ca(OH)_2 \rightarrow C-S-H \text{ (Calcium Silicate Hydrate gel)}$$
- This C-S-H gel is the **primary strength-giving phase** in hardened concrete.

**Step 3: Blending with Clinker**
- RHA is ground to a fineness of **> 300 m²/kg (Blaine's specific surface area)**.
- Mixed with Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) clinker in proportions of **10–30% by mass**.
- A small amount of gypsum (3–5%) is added to control setting time.

**Step 4: Resulting Cement Types**
- **Portland Pozzolana Cement (PPC)** — IS 1489 Part 1 standard.
- **Calcium Silicate Cement** — high-performance cement.
- **Geopolymer Cement** — 100% RHA + alkaline activator (zero clinker, low CO₂ footprint).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| **Silica Yield** | $\eta = \frac{M_{SiO_2}}{M_{RHA}} \times 100$ | Mass of silica recovered per unit mass of ash | % |
| **Pozzolanic Activity Index (PAI)** | $PAI = \frac{f_s}{f_c} \times 100$ | Strength of RHA-cement mortar / strength of OPC mortar at 28 days | % |
| **Compressive Strength** | $f_c = \frac{P}{A}$ | Load at failure / cross-sectional area | N/mm² or MPa |
| **Loss on Ignition (LOI)** | $LOI = \frac{m_1 - m_2}{m_1} \times 100$ | $\vert m_1 - m_2 \vert / m_1$ ratio of mass loss after burning | % |
| **Fineness (Blaine)** | $S = \frac{K \cdot \sqrt{t}}{V}$ | Specific surface area from air permeability | m²/kg |
| **Cement Replacement Ratio** | $R = \frac{M_{RHA}}{M_{clinker} + M_{RHA}} \times 100$ | Mass fraction of RHA in blended cement | % |
| **Setting Time Ratio** | $R_t = \frac{t_{RHA}}{t_{OPC}}$ | Relative setting time w.r.t. OPC | Dimensionless |
| **CO₂ Reduction** | $\Delta CO_2 = \frac{M_{RHA}}{M_{clinker}} \times 0.9$ | Approx. 0.9 tonnes CO₂ saved per tonne clinker replaced | tonnes |

> [!NOTE]
> **ASTM C618** specifies that for Class F pozzolans (RHA falls in this category), the sum of $\vert SiO_2 \vert + \vert Al_2O_3 \vert + \vert Fe_2O_3 \vert$ must be **> 70%**, and LOI must be **< 10%** for structural-grade applications.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Rice Husk Silica/Cement |
|--------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Civil Construction** | PPC for mass concreting (dams, bridges) — reduces thermal cracking |
| **High-Performance Concrete** | RHA + silica fume → ultra-high-strength concrete (UHSC) |
| **Geopolymer Concrete** | 100% RHA + NaOH/Na₂SiO₃ → low-carbon cement |
| **Catalyst Supports** | Mesoporous silica from RHA for petroleum refining |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Nano-silica as drug carrier and excipient |
| **Electronics** | High-purity SiO₂ for semiconductor insulation |
| **Agriculture** | Lightweight RHA-based soil conditioners and fertilizers |
| **Cement Industry** | Reduces CO₂ emissions by **~0.9 tonnes per tonne of clinker replaced** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Theoretical Maximum Silica Yield from Rice Husk

**Problem Setup:** A rice mill produces 10,000 kg of rice husk per day. The silica content in the husk is determined to be 20% by dry weight. Calculate the theoretical mass of pure silica that can be extracted if the recovery efficiency is 85%.

**Step 1: Mass of dry rice husk (MRH)**

Assuming moisture content is 8%, the dry mass of rice husk is:

$$M_{RH,dry} = M_{RH,wet} \times (1 - \text{moisture content})$$

$$M_{RH,dry} = 10{,}000 \times (1 - 0.08) = 9{,}200 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2: Theoretical silica mass from dry husk**

$$M_{SiO_2,theoretical} = M_{RH,dry} \times \% SiO_2$$

$$M_{SiO_2,theoretical} = 9{,}200 \times 0.20 = 1{,}840 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3: Actual silica mass after recovery losses**

$$M_{SiO_2,actual} = M_{SiO_2,theoretical} \times \eta_{recovery}$$

$$M_{SiO_2,actual} = 1{,}840 \times 0.85 = 1{,}564 \text{ kg}$$

**Result:** Approximately **1,564 kg** of pure silica can be extracted daily from 10,000 kg of wet rice husk at 85% recovery.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Pozzolanic Activity Index (PAI) Calculation

**Problem Setup:** A blended cement contains 20% RHA and 80% OPC clinker. The 28-day compressive strength of the RHA-blended cement mortar is 38 MPa, while that of pure OPC mortar is 45 MPa. Determine whether the blend qualifies as a structural pozzolan per ASTM C618.

**Step 1: Apply PAI formula**

$$PAI = \frac{f_{s,RHA}}{f_{s,OPC}} \times 100$$

$$PAI = \frac{38}{45} \times 100 = 84.44\%$$

**Step 2: Compare with ASTM C618 threshold**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ASTM C618 Specification:** For Class F pozzolan, PAI must be **$\geq$ 75%** of OPC strength at 28 days.

**Step 3: Conclusion**

Since $PAI = 84.44\% \geq 75\%$, the **RHA qualifies** as a Class F pozzolan and is suitable for structural concrete applications.

## 3.3 Derivation III: CO₂ Emission Reduction in Cement Production

**Problem Setup:** A cement plant produces 1,000 tonnes/day of PPC with 20% RHA replacement. Calculate the daily CO₂ emission reduction.

**Step 1: Mass of RHA used**

$$M_{RHA} = 1{,}000 \times 0.20 = 200 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**Step 2: Equivalent clinker mass replaced**

$$M_{clinker,replaced} = M_{RHA} = 200 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**Step 3: CO₂ emission factor for clinker production**

Average CO₂ emission per tonne of clinker production = **0.9 tonnes CO₂/tonne clinker**

**Step 4: Daily CO₂ reduction**

$$\Delta CO_2 = M_{clinker,replaced} \times EF_{CO_2}$$

$$\Delta CO_2 = 200 \times 0.9 = 180 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{/day}$$

**Step 5: Annual CO₂ reduction (assuming 300 operating days)**

$$\Delta CO_{2,annual} = 180 \times 300 = 54{,}000 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{/year}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Combustion Methods

| Parameter | Controlled Combustion (Furnace) | Uncontrolled Combustion (Open) | Fluidized Bed Combustion |
|-----------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Temperature** | 500–700°C | 800–1200°C | 600–750°C |
| **Silica Form** | Amorphous (reactive) | Crystalline (inert) | Amorphous (reactive) |
| **Color of Ash** | Grey to Black | White to Grey | Grey to Black |
| **LOI (%)** | 5–10% | < 2% | 3–8% |
| **Pozzolanic Activity** | High | Low | High |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Low | High |
| **KTU Exam Tip** | **Most preferred** | **Avoid for cement** | Industrial scale |

## 3.5 Derivation IV: Mass Balance for Silica Extraction via Alkali Route

Starting from 100 kg of acid-leached RHA (95% SiO₂ content):

**Step 1: Mass of SiO₂ in ash**

$$M_{SiO_2} = 100 \times 0.95 = 95 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2: Moles of SiO₂** (Molar mass of SiO₂ = 60 g/mol)

$$n_{SiO_2} = \frac{95{,}000}{60} = 1{,}583.33 \text{ moles}$$

**Step 3: Reaction stoichiometry**

$$SiO_2 + 2NaOH \rightarrow Na_2SiO_3 + H_2O$$

For 1 mole of SiO₂, 2 moles of NaOH are required.

$$n_{NaOH,required} = 2 \times 1{,}583.33 = 3{,}166.67 \text{ moles}$$

**Step 4: Mass of NaOH required** (Molar mass = 40 g/mol)

$$M_{NaOH} = 3{,}166.67 \times 40 = 126{,}666.67 \text{ g} = 126.67 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5: Mass of Na₂SiO₃ produced** (Molar mass = 122 g/mol)

$$M_{Na_2SiO_3} = 1{,}583.33 \times 122 = 193{,}166.67 \text{ g} = 193.17 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6: Precipitation with H₂SO₄**

$$Na_2SiO_3 + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow SiO_2 \downarrow + Na_2SO_4 + H_2O$$

The theoretical mass of precipitated SiO₂ = **95 kg** (recovered silica).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram: Silica Production from Rice Husk

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Rice Husk Collection] --> B[Washing with Deionized Water]
    B --> C[Drying at 110C for 12 Hours]
    C --> D{Combustion Method}
    D --> E[Controlled Furnace at 600C]
    D --> F[Fluidized Bed Reactor]
    D --> G[Open Field Burning]
    E --> H[Amorphous RHA - Grey Black]
    F --> H
    G --> I[Crystalline RHA - White]
    H --> J[Acid Leaching with 2M HCl at 80C]
    J --> K[Filtration and Washing pH 7]
    K --> L[Alkali Digestion with 2M NaOH at 90C]
    L --> M[Sodium Silicate Solution]
    M --> N[Precipitation with H2SO4 at pH 4 to 5]
    N --> O[Silica Gel]
    O --> P[Aging and Washing]
    P --> Q[Drying at 120C]
    Q --> R[Calcination at 700C]
    R --> S[Pure Amorphous Silica Powder]
    I --> T[Not Suitable for Cement]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram: Cement Production from Rice Husk Ash

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Rice Husk] --> B[Cleaning and Drying]
    B --> C[Controlled Combustion at 600 to 700C]
    C --> D[Amorphous RHA]
    D --> E[Grinding to Blaine 300 m2 per kg]
    E --> F[Blending with OPC Clinker]
    F --> G{Blending Ratio}
    G --> H[10 percent RHA - Standard PPC]
    G --> I[20 to 30 percent RHA - High Pozzolanic PPC]
    G --> J[100 percent RHA - Geopolymer Cement]
    H --> K[Addition of 3 to 5 percent Gypsum]
    I --> K
    K --> L[Inter-grinding or Blending]
    L --> M[Portland Pozzolana Cement PPC]
    J --> N[Addition of NaOH and Na2SiO3 Activator]
    N --> O[Geopolymer Cement]
    M --> P[Quality Testing as per IS 1489]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Packaging and Dispatch]
```

    B --> C{Presence of RHA Amorphous SiO2}
